timeline.title: 1. Image Preprocessing Workflow
timeline.summary: ## Segmentation Test - MESMER Codes are in Github Test files are here: P:\\h30492\\farkkilab2\\7_TLS\\Segmentation_test\\STARDIST_AND_MESMER Based on Ziqi, Zhihan and Ada's work: - Multichannel TIFF images were processed using: - Gamma correction (gamma=γ) to enhance contrast. - Unsharp masking (sigma=σ, strength=s) to sharpen nuclear features. - Channels (e.g., DAPI, membrane) were stacked into a 3D array format and reshaped to comply with MESMER's input shape: (1, H, W, C).

timeline.title: 2. Tiling Strategy
timeline.summary: - Images were divided into overlapping tiles using a fixed tile size and dynamically computed overlaps to ensure full coverage. - This produced a tile array of shape (B, num_x, num_y, tile_x, tile_y, C).

timeline.title: 3. Filtering Background Tiles with GMM and Other Metrics
timeline.summary: To optimize nuclear segmentation by avoiding background-only regions, several strategies were tested for identifying and excluding low-information tiles before running MESMER. These methods aimed to reduce unnecessary computation and improve segmentation quality.

timeline.title: 3.1 Baseline Thresholding with Mean Intensity
timeline.summary: - Method: Calculated the mean pixel intensity of each tile on the DAPI channel (channel_idx=0). - Threshold: Tiles with mean \< 3500 were excluded from segmentation. - Outcome: Performed well on images with consistent staining but failed in cases with uneven illumination or high signal heterogeneity.

timeline.title: 3.2 Comparison with Sum-Based Filtering
timeline.summary: - Method: Used np.sum() of DAPI intensity values instead of the mean. - Observation: Performed poorly despite identical tile sizes. Since np.sum scales linearly with total intensity, it was more sensitive to background noise and illumination bias, leading to misclassification of low-content tiles. - Conclusion: np.mean was more robust due to normalization by pixel count.

timeline.title: 3.3 Feature Combination: Mean + Standard Deviation
timeline.summary: - Method: Combined mean and standard deviation (std) of DAPI intensities per tile into 2D feature vectors. - Goal: Capture both brightness and texture/variance of tiles. - Input to GMM: X = \[\[mean₁, std₁\], \[mean₂, std₂\], \..., \[meanₙ, stdₙ\]\] - Result: Improved separation of background vs. foreground in heterogeneous images.

timeline.title: 3.4 Median Intensity Filtering
timeline.summary: - Trial: Replaced mean with median intensity as a more outlier-robust statistic. - Result: Median has a close result compared to mthe ean.

timeline.title: 3.5 Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) Clustering
timeline.summary: - Method: Applied sklearn.mixture.GaussianMixture to intensity features (mean or \[mean + std\]) across all tiles. - Parameters: - n_components = 3 (assumed: background, low-signal, high-signal) - random_state = 0 for reproducibility - Label Assignment: - Automatically determined the cluster with the lowest mean as background (via filter_tiles_by_mean_intensity_gmm_auto_background) - Background labels were excluded from downstream prediction height="3.0416666666666665in"}

protocols.title: Nano -Pick: A Novel Method Enables Spatially
steps: Most patients are diagnosed at an advanced stage, resulting in a poor prognosis.
Although some targeted therapies have made good progress, immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) still show limited efficacy in HGSC treatment.
This is closely associated with the high heterogeneity and unique immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment (TME) of HGSC.
As local immune centers in the TME, tertiary lymphoid structures (TLSs) contain diverse types of immune cells and play a crucial role in the anti-tumor immune process.
Nonetheless, the spatial immune landscape of TLSs and key gene expression signatures affecting prognosis have not been fully elucidated.
In recent years, spatially resolved gene expression profiling of clinical samples has provided insights into the transcriptome at high resolution.

However, despite revealing a series of response mechanisms of anti-tumor immunity , these methods still have many limitations in non-single cell resolution, specific tissue sample types, and high cost.
Here in this thesis, I establish ed Nano-Pick, a novel method based on precise microtissue extraction and the nCounter analysis system.
Nano-Pick enables cost- effective and rapid spatial gene expression profiling using formalin-fixed paraffin- embedded (FFPE) samples.
All TLSs-enriched FFPE slides were obtained from patient-derived HGSC samples at Helsinki University Hospital (HUH).
With the help of tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CycIF), a highly multiplexed imaging method, selected regions of interests (ROIs) were annotated in different zones of TLSs.
These ROIs were accurately transferred to adjacent tissue sections, guiding the target region selection for microtissue extraction.

document_index.title: 1.Tuulia Bruno's Talk summary (10 min)

document_index.title: Cell Phenotyping TLS-IDS
document_index.excerpt: Cell Phenotyping TLS-IDS The binary classification of TLSs into immature TLSs (iTLSs)/early TLSs (eTLSs) and mature TLSs (mTLSs) 1) early TLSs (eTLSs), in which lymphocytes aggregate diffusely and CD21+ cells are scarce, and 2) follicle-formed mature TLSs, in which the follicular morphology of SLOs and CD21+ follicular DCs (FDCs) are distributed in a reticular pattern (17). Mature TLSs (mTLSs) with GCs and DCs reportedly represent privileged sites for local antigen presentation and contribute to the generation of tumor-targeting effector T cells and B cell Nielsen et al. discovered that typical markers of antigen-presenting cells, such as MHC class I/II, CD40, CD80, and CD86, were expressed on TLS-associated B cells in HGSOC. document_index.title: Filtering empty tiles by maximum intensity of the tile is not working, too many of them have max=65535. Use median instead.
document_index.excerpt: Filtering empty tiles by maximum intensity of the tile is not working, too many of them have max=65535. Use median instead. Threshold (median DAPI intensity per tile) = 2500, Worked but not perfect. However the running time on empty tiles and non-empty tiles are the same: This image has 2963=1827 tiles, each 25002500, each tile will take <100 second to segment (I will use 95s to calculate here), so the total time will be 48.2h. If we drop 364 empty tiles, the time will reduced by 9.6h, so the total time will be 38.6h.

document_index.title: Material and Methods
document_index.excerpt: Samples were stained consecutively using a panel of validated antibodies (IDS samples: Table XX, PDS and milky-spots samples: Table XX ) and scanned on a RareCyte CyteFinder system according to the standard t-CyCIF protocol (CITE) at a resolution of 0.65 um pixel with 2x2 binning. Raw image files underwent illumination correction using the BaSiC tool. Stitching and registration were performed utilizing ASHLAR (v1.17.0) (CITE) to generate unified, multi-channel OME-TIFF images. Nuclear segmentation was performed using Mesmer (CITE), a deep learning-based segmentation algorithm. To address low signal-to-noise ratios (SNR) and mitigate tissue autofluorescence, a morphological white top-hat filter was applied to a subset of the lineage marker channels (Table XX) across all images. document_index.excerpt: Nano -Pick: A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer Translational Medicine (TRANSMED) High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) is the most aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer. Most patients are diagnosed at an advanced stage, resulting in a poor prognosis. Although some targeted therapies have made good progress, immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) still show limited efficacy in HGSC treatment. This is closely associated with the high heterogeneity and unique immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment (TME) of HGSC. As local immune centers in the TME, tertiary lymphoid structures (TLSs) contain diverse types of immune cells and play a crucial role in the anti-tumor immune process. document_index.excerpt: Nano -Pick: A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer Ovarian cancer ranks as one of the deadliest forms of gynecological malignancies, affecting over 300,000 women globally each year. High -grade serous carcinoma (HGSC), the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer, typically presents with a poor prognosis. Due to the absence of distinct symptoms in the early stages and the high dissemination rate of HGSC, 75% of HGSC patients are diagnosed at stage III or IV according to the FIGO staging system . Despite extensive efforts, optimal cytoreduction alone with various combinations of chemotherapy cannot significantly improve the survival rate of patients with advanced HGSC . document_index.title: This is the folder for the 7_TLS project.
document_index.excerpt: This is the folder for the 7_TLS project. Members: Ada Junquera, Zhihan Liang, Hanna Elomaa 1. Management & Planning The Gantt chart is located here. Other guidelines and project tasks can 2. Methods & Experiments Here we have protocols and other experimental files. In the data folder, we have Xenium data and segmentation test results. Manuscript ideas and figures are assigned to the figures folder. Meeting notes, personal notes, and presentation slides are here. 5. Writing & Dissemination Conference abstracts, TLS project manuscript plan, and all other files related to paper writing can be found here. We have master\'s thesis and other outdated files here.

vector_chunks.text:
## Sheet: Marker options
Scope of milky spot tCycIF panel
> to possibly find milky spot-like structures from benign/borderline omentum samples
> different characteristics between milky spots and PDS omentum Lagg?
> panel designed based on previous literature characterizing MS
MARKER TYPE TARGET WHY? MILKY SPOTS LAGG iTLS mTLS LIMITATIONS AVAILABLE CHANNEL
CD3 Immune cell T cells to identify immune structures, helping LTi detection +/-, scattered +, scattered +, zone +, around GC yes 555.0
CD4 Immune cell T helper cells to identify immune structures, to study T cell subtypes +/-, scattered +, scattered +, zone +, around GC unspec. targeting to macrophages yes 488, 555
CD8 Immune cell T cytotoxic cells to identify immune structures, to study T cell subtypes +/-, scattered +, scattered +, zone +, around GC yes 633.0
CD20 Immune cell All B cells to identify immune structures most +, scattered most +, scattered +, zone +, in GC yes 633.0
IBA-1 Immune cell All macrophages to distinguish MS from Lagg +, scattered +/-, scattered +, scattered +, scattered yes 488 750
CD163 Immune cell M2-like macrophages to study M1/M2 polarization states of macrophages (pro-/anti-inflammatory)? +, scattered +/-, scattered +, scattered +, scattered may not be exlusive for M2-macrophages yes 488.0
HLA-DPB2 (MHCII) Immune cell MHCII protein, in APCs (M1-macrophages, DCs, some B cells) Essential for CD4+ activation. MS lack DCs (=no antigen presenting capability) > to distinguish MS from Lagg. +/-, scattered +/-, scattered +/-, scattered +/-, scattered yes 555, 647

CD21 Immune cell follicular dendritic cells to differentiate iTLS from Lagg (might be expressed also in Lagg but should be weaker) - +/-, scattered +, scattered +, in GC possibly in some B cells, might not express in all iTS (Sun 2024 rw) yes 647 488
Vimentin Stroma Stromal cells To compartmentalize stromal areas 750.0
PDPN Stroma Some stromal fibroblasts and fibroblasts in FRCs to distinguish MS from Lagg +/-, in FRCs around lymphocytes, less active than in Lagg (need to be checked from the literature) +/-, scattered, weaker than in MS (or other way around, need to be checked) + + unspec targeting to endothelial and epithelial cells, may be expressed in Lagg but should be weaker than in MS no 555 (new)
CD31 Stroma Blood vessels/endothelial cells To find blood vessels around immune structures + +. in the middle and borders +, in borders +, in borders +, in borders yes 647.0
HEV (MECA-79) Stroma High endothelial venules to distinguish Lagg from other structures most + most - +/- + yes 488.0
Ki-67 Functional Proliferating cells/proliferation state to find active cells, to possibly find Lagg that are maturating to iTLS and iTLS that are maturating to mTLS +/-, scattered +/-, scattered +/-, scattered +/-, in GC yes 488 647
PD1 (PDCD1) Functional PD-1 immune checkpoint on T cells to investigate the immunosuppressivity of structures +/-. scattered +/-. scattered +/-. scattered in T zone +/-, scattered around GC yes 647.0
BCL6 Transcription factor Chemokine of GC to identify GCs (=mTLS) - - - +/-, in GC yes unconj

### **Small initial point** 

There is an interesting talk tomorrow, Zhihan will make a recording so
that we can check together:

### Speaker: Tullia Bruno, PhD, University of Pittsburgh and UPMC Hillman Cancer Center

### Talk Title: *\"The Activity of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in High-Grade Serous Ovarian Cancer Is Governed by Site, Stroma, and Cellular Interactions\"*

### Recording: Zhihan will record the session for team review, Zoom Link: [[Meeting Link]{.underline}](https://us02web.zoom.us/j/82392041446?pwd=IKzb4au8t203iZSpYMgJD2MpMcUFJf.1)

- **The recording is [here
 > <https://datacloud.helsinki.fi/index.php/s/HMdjyeoWPG4Pdri>]{.mark}**

### **Action Items and Discussion Points**

#### **1. Spatial Tumor Microenvironment (TME) Exploration**

- **New Experiments vs. Existing Data:\
 > **Discussed whether to conduct new experiments or utilize existing
 > data. We concluded that we will try to perform new experiments for
 > a cohort of IDS patients. In this regard, we have two main
 > interesting points coming out of the discussion:

 - **HRD Sample Updates:\
 > **Treatment protocol was adjusted for HRD samples---now with
 > only 3 chemotherapy cycles. We should anticipate a new batch
 > of HRD samples with higher tumor percentages.

 - **Discussion of whether to include low tumor purity samples:**

 - **Questions:** May these had and already dismantled TLSs?.
 > Joona will be consulted about exploring different
 > timepoints in distinct samples (formation, maturation,
 > dismantling).

#### **2. Next Steps on Profiling and Sample Selection**

- **Ada's Tasks:**

 - Profile all IDS (may narrow to HRP).

 - Select samples for HGSC, endometroid carcinoma, and omentum.

 - For these samples we will plan a panel similar to SPACE,

**[GeoMx strategic proposal - 20/11]{.underline}**

### **[Research Objective:]{.underline}**

We aim to dissect the molecular interactions in various areas within the
TLSs (Tfh-gc b cells and myeloid - T cell interactions)

### **[Experimental Design Overview:]{.underline}**

**Sample Set Proposal:** One of them has long PFS and the other, short
PFS

![](media/image1.png){width="6.046875546806649in"
height="0.7917344706911636in"}

**Sample Selection Criteria:**[BRCAwt, multiple TLS, HGSC IDS
samples]{.underline}

**Sample Exclusion criteria:** [BRCAmut, only single TLS, high fat
content, PDS samples, short follow-up,]{.underline} Pre-Post NACT sample
set slides where one of the pairs contained no TLS.

- [BRCAmut TLS multiple TLS]{.underline}s: S123 was discarded (BRCA1)
 > was good, but discarded and S128 (gBRCA1) had extremely poor tumor
 > content (almost all of it was fat)
 > ![](media/image2.png){width="1.381459973753281in"
 > height="1.0783847331583551in"}

**[Sample Processing:]{.underline}**

FFPE blocks are in the lab and we need to section them:

- [2 samples located in one slide.]{.underline} Mind that the
 > scannable area of the slide is 35.3x14.1 mm, so tissues need to be
 > placed accordingly

- Ideally we need at least 6 of these:

 - 1 slide for tCycIF and 1 slide for GeoMx (2)

 - 1 back-up for both tCycIF and GeoMx (2)

 - Extra-slides for additional/future experiments or validations
 > (2)

**[ROI selection strategy:]{.underline}**

- [tCycIF will be performed with this panel:]{.underline}

 - GC B cells - CD20, Ki67

 - TFh cells: Bcl-6, CD3D

 - Myeloid cells- IBA1

 - Cancer cells - PanCk

 - Stromal cells - Vimentin

- [Regions will be manually selected based on tCycIF results following
 > the pre-established pipeline.

vector_chunks.source_file: 4. Meetings & Updates/Meeting_notes/2025/15.04.25 Segmentation issues - Anniina, Ada, Hanna, Ziqi, Zhihan.md
vector_chunks.text:
**Meeting Notes 15042025**

**Topic:** TLS Segmentation Issues\
**Date:** 15.04.2025\
**Attendees:** Anniina, Ada, Hanna, Zhihan, Ziqi

**1. TLS Segmentation Strategy**

- **Mesmer vs. Stardist:**

 - Mesmer shows better performance with longer running times
 compared to Stardist.

 - Mesmer runs on small cropped tiles; additional resources are
 needed to apply it to full-slide images.

 - Tile stitching consideration: ensure proper handling of cells in
 overlapping regions.

 - Black areas (bubbles) can cause segmentation error -- better
 skipping and need to filter or avoid black tiles.

- **Benefits of Mesmer:**

 - Performs more accurately on thicker tissue sections.

 - This improved performance will benefit future projects,
 including proteopicks using thicker sections.

- **Optimization Plan:**

 - Ziqi will spend one week post-Easter to optimize and upgrade the
 Mesmer-based segmentation pipeline. Lina has scripts that could
 support this process.

- **Backup Plan:**

 - If whole-slide segmentation is not feasible, fallback will be to
 crop individual TLS regions and segment them using Mesmer
 separately.

**2. Tissue Section Thickness Considerations**

- **4 μm sections:**

 - Used in SPACE project, better but still suboptimal for
 segmentation of mature TLS structures.

- **5 μm sections (current study):**

 - Chosen to collect more material for GeoMx analysis, causing
 segmentation issues in dense areas like TLSs.

- **General Note:**

 - Thinner sections are preferable for H&E staining.

 - Thicker sections are more suitable for RNA/protein profiling.

 - A future reporting system is applied to track section thickness
 (in μm) used per experiment.

**3. Action Points & Next Steps**

- By **next Friday**, Ziqi will assess feasibility of Mesmer WSI

Timeline expected:

**Possible future implementations for DBSCAN**

1. Close contact thresholding

2. apply this to the HE

**EF-responses**

1.Quantify this phenomena

2.Clinical differences

3.Phenotypes of these B cells? -\> xenium?

**NOTES FOR tCycIF analyses-**

1. **Are my B-cell clusters active or just passive aggregates?**

2. **\"Is the immune system successfully \'shipping\' cells from the
 > TLS to the Tumor?\"**

3. **\"What is the role of those B-cells sitting exactly at the tumor
 > interface?\"**

4. **\"Where are the \'Cold Zones\' that the immune system can\'t
 > reach?\"**

![](media/image1.png){width="6.299212598425197in"
height="3.4027777777777777in"}

**https://www.researchgate.net/publication/372203129_Germinal_center-dependent_and\_-independent_immune_responses_of_tumor-infiltrating_B_cells_in_human_cancers**

**IMMUNE INFILTRATION METRICS**

- **Normalized Mixing Score (NMS):** Measures the degree of \"mixing\"
 > between two cell types (e.g., CD8+ T cells and Cytokeratin+ tumor
 > cells).

- **Tumor-Immune Partitioning & Clustering (TIPC):** A newer algorithm
 > that uses hexagonal tessellation to partition the tissue into
 > sub-regions. It classifies regions as \"hot/infiltrated\" or
 > \"cold/excluded\" based on the ratio and clustering of immune vs.
 > tumor cells within each hexagon.

**The \"Invasive Front\" Distance:** 1. Define the tumor boundary (using
your Tumor label coordinates).\
2. Measure the distance from each **CD8** and **CD4** cell to the
nearest **B-cell cluster**.\
3. Measure the distance from those same T-cells to the **Tumor
boundary**.\
4. **Metric:** The **\"Relay Ratio.\"** Are your T-cells concentrated
*between* the TLS and the tumor? This indicates the TLS is successfully
\"seeding\" the tumor.

**Intratumoral Mixing Score:** Use the ConvexHull logic on your
**Tumor** cells. Then, count how many **Myeloid** and **CD8** cells fall
inside that Hull.

- *Infiltration Degree* =
 > \$\\frac{N\_{CD8\\\_Inside}}{Area\_{Tumor\\\_Hull}}\$

e tumor.

**Intratumoral Mixing Score:** Use the ConvexHull logic on your
**Tumor** cells. Then, count how many **Myeloid** and **CD8** cells fall
inside that Hull.

- *Infiltration Degree* =
 > \$\\frac{N\_{CD8\\\_Inside}}{Area\_{Tumor\\\_Hull}}\$

**TLS-shape and proximity metrics**

- **Maturity:**For each B-cell cluster, calculate the percentage of
 > cells positive for BCL6 and Ki67.*High BCL6+/Ki67+ count* = Active
 > Secondary Follicle (highly prognostic).

- Presence of CD21 within a B-cell cluster confirms a \"Primary\" or
 > \"Secondary\" follicle rather than a loose aggregate

- **TLS Density:** Total number of TLS divided by the total tissue
 > area

- **TLS** shape and eccentricity

 - **Area-to-Perimeter Ratio** or **Circularity**

 - **High Circularity: Suggests a mature, secondary follicle (Stage
 > 3).**

 - **(Amoeboid): Suggests an early, disorganized B cell aggregate
 > (Stage 1). THIS IS DONE THROUGH SCIMAP CONVEXHULL**

- **Define Intra/Peritumoral:** TLS within a defined buffer (usually
 > **2 mm**) from the tumor margin.

- **Proximity to Tumor (NND):** The **Nearest Neighbor Distance** from
 > the center of each TLS to the nearest tumor cell boundary

**TLS definition**

- **The T-cell Recruitment Metric:** Count how many \$CD4\^+\$ and
 > \$CD8\^+\$ T cells fall within a 50µm buffer of your DBSCAN B cell
 > clusters**.**

**TLS-composition explorationn**

- **B-T Zone Polarization:** Use the Centroid Distance between the
 > B-cell cluster and the T-cell cluster. In mature TLS, these zones
 > are spatially segregated but adjacent.

- **Shannon Entropy (Spatial):** Measures the randomness of cell
 > distribution within the TLS. Lower entropy usually indicates a
 > more organized, \"mature\" TLS structure.

r. In mature TLS, these zones
 > are spatially segregated but adjacent.

- **Shannon Entropy (Spatial):** Measures the randomness of cell
 > distribution within the TLS. Lower entropy usually indicates a
 > more organized, \"mature\" TLS structure.

- Calculate the density of HEV1 and CD31 cells within or immediately
 > adjacent (e.g., within 50µm) to the cluster.

**Important!! Correlate CD8 to
CD20https://[[www.nature.com/articles/s41586-019-1914-8]{.underline}](http://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-019-1914-8)**

**\####### EF-response**

### The \"Neighborhood\" Analysis (Cell-to-Cell)

Look at the immediate neighbors of these interface B cells. Use a 30 µm
radius and check for:

- B cell + Myeloid: Suggests antigen presentation or a \"suppressive\"
 > niche if those myeloid cells are \$CD163\^+\$ (M2 macrophages).

- B cell + CD8: Suggests the B cells are acting as local
 > antigen-presenting cells to boost T-cell killing at the edge

Are they proliferating (\$Ki67\^+\$)? If B cells at the interface are
dividing, it suggests the tumor edge is a site of active immune
expansion, not just a passive graveyard.

Are they mature (\$BCL6\^+\$)? BCL6 is usually in the germinal center.
If you find \$BCL6\^+\$ B cells \"leaking\" into the tumor interface, it
indicates an extremely aggressive local immune response

**What is the relevance of studying the normal omentum?**

Omental milky spots (MSs) are pre-existing serosal immune niches that
appear in the peritoneal cavity and already contain B cells,
macrophages, HEV-like vessels, and CXCL13⁺ stromal/myeloid cells.

TLSs (tertiary lymphoid structures) in OvCa are linked to stronger local
immunity (CD8⁺ T cells, B cell activity) and often better prognosis.

neal cavity and already contain B cells,
macrophages, HEV-like vessels, and CXCL13⁺ stromal/myeloid cells.

TLSs (tertiary lymphoid structures) in OvCa are linked to stronger local
immunity (CD8⁺ T cells, B cell activity) and often better prognosis.
Biologically, TLSs share features with SLOs (B/T zoning, HEVs, sometimes
germinal centers).

**Are omental MSs the "seeds" of TLSs in ovarian cancer metastases?**

1. Are MSs diverse or homogeneous in their composition and spatial
 > organization?

 a. Do transitional "reactive MSs" exist --- same topology as MS but
 > GC-maturation

 b. Any MSs displays TLS-like spatial feature? Eg B-T cell
 > segregation

 c. Are MSs the same as LAgg?

 d. Can we find common and different consistent features across all
 > MSs/TLSs

 - FDC-like cells (CD21⁺/CD35⁺) often absent in MSs, appear in
 > TLSs.

2. How do MSs distribute? Are TLSs architecturally continuous with the
 > anatomical locations of pre-existing MSs in tumor-free omentum? eg
 > proximity to vessels, stromal cells

3. Can we already see patient-associated diversity in points 1 and 2?

CD20 BCL6 CD4 CD3D CD8 CD11c IBA1 Vimentin CD31 aSMA

**How do HRP/HRD PDS differ in TLS composition?**

[Baseline info:]{.underline}

Development and organization of omental milky spots

vector_chunks.text:
**[TLS analyses from H&E and CycIF whole slide images]{.underline}**

# BACKGROUND

- **TLS general**

 - TLSs orchestrate effective antitumor immunity because of
 > crosstalk of B and T cells

 - B cells undergo affinity maturation in GC

 - Low tumor mutational burden 🡪 less CD8+ cells and B cell
 > recruitment 🡪 less intratumoral TLS

 - B cells in TLS are critical in controlling tumor development and
 > immunotherapy response (Teillaud 2024)

 - B cells are key hallmarks of TLS. Part of Lagg may be T cell+/B
 > cell- but cannot be developed into mature TLS?

 - CD23 and CD21 strongly expressed in mature TLS (some low level
 > expression may be found also in other)

 - T cell-B cell segregation is dependent on increased secretion of
 > lymphoid chemokines (Teillaud 2024)

 - CCL19 or CCL21-CCR7 axis for T cell zone segregation

 - CXCL13-CXCR5 axis for B cell zone

 - mature TLS can exist in tumor sites that are not normally
 > thought to be infiltrated by immune cells (Petitprez)

 - TLS develop in non-lymphoid tissues in inflamed microenvironment

 - "Current research indicates that the formation of TLSs is
 > primarily driven by persistent antigenic stimulation
 > within the chronic inflammatory microenvironment \[13\]
 > (Su 2025 rw)

 - "the triggers and processes involved in the origin and
 > development of TLSs have become increasingly clear \[16,
 > 17\]" (Su 2025 rw)

 - ["the formation of TLSs]{.underline} originates from the
 > homing of lymphoid tissue\
 > inducer (LTi) cells or their substitute cells to
 > inflammatory sites \[10\]." (Su 2025 rw) **→ LTi only in
 > TLS structures, not in milky spots**

 - TLS potentially enhance patients's survival and advance the
 > response to ICB therapy (Su 2025 rw)

- **The role of cell types in TLS (Su 2025 rw):**

 - TLS potentially enhance patients's survival and advance the
 > response to ICB therapy (Su 2025 rw)

- **The role of cell types in TLS (Su 2025 rw):**

 - CD4+ T cells: the most prevalent T cell type in T cell zone \>
 > activation of naive B cells, formation of GC by facilitating
 > antigen presentation

 - DCs: activate initial CD8+ T cells

 - CD8+ T cells: kill tumor cells, secrete effector molecules
 > (granzyme B etc.)

 - CD20+ B cell follicles within GC:s: sites for B cell
 > proliferation and antibody class transformation

 - CD21+ FDCs: selection of memory B cells during GC reactions

 - HEVs: recruit lymphocytes by secreting chemokines

 - FRC: support TLS structure, anchor it to inflamed tissue to
 > ensure effective local immune response

- **TLS detection and classification**

 - **Su et al rw 2025:**

 - **E-TLS: early TLS**

 - **PFL-TLSs: primary follicle-like tertiary lymphoid
 > structures;**

 - **SFL-TLSs: secondary follicle-like tertiary lymphoid
 > structures**

> ![](media/image14.png){width="3.3802088801399823in"
> height="4.07084864391951in"}

- T&B cell localization/distribution:

 - **Lagg** = mixed aggregates of T and B cells (or only T cells)

 - **Immature TLS** = T cell and B cell zones (might be partially
 > mixed), no GC

 - **Mature TLS** = B cells forming a clear follicle and GC is in
 > the middle, T cells are usually in the borders

- Kroeger 2016: four types based on size, cellular composition and
 > degree of organization:

 - **type I:** small, 20-50 cells, compact, T and B and
 > occasionally DCs → like Lagg

 - **type II:** 100- \>1000 cells, T and B cells, diffuse, lacked

vector_chunks.text:
- Kroeger 2016: four types based on size, cellular composition and
 > degree of organization:

 - **type I:** small, 20-50 cells, compact, T and B and
 > occasionally DCs → like Lagg

 - **type II:** 100- \>1000 cells, T and B cells, diffuse, lacked
 > discrete zones or follicles like Lagg? bigger, not discrete
 > zones so wouldnät refer to iTLS but could these still be iTLS?

 - **type III:** fully developed TLS, B cell follicles with GC-like
 > structures, discrete T cell zone, DCs and HEV-like vesselsl→
 > like mTLS

 - **type IV:** 100-300 B cells and DC organized into follicles
 > with few T cells, not clear T cell zones 🡪 as primarily found
 > in omentum, these were speculated to be mostly normal milky
 > spots → like milky spots

- Teillaud 2024:

 - **Lymphoid aggregate:** few T and B cells, occasional DCs, no
 > fDCs, minimal organization

 - **Immature TLS** (=follicle-like TLS): dense and organized T
 > cells, B cells and FDCs with distinct T cell and B cell zones,
 > no GC

 - **Mature TLS** (secondary follicle-like TLS): T cell-rich areas
 > (mainly T cells and mature dendritic cells (DCs)) adjacent to
 > B cell-rich areas containing a germinal centre (mainly B
 > cells, FDCs, follicular helper T cells (TFH cells) and
 > macrophages), GCs and HEVs, full functional capacity by
 > promoting T and B cell activation 🡪 lead to TLS expansion
 > through cell proliferation and recruitment

 - **Laggs and TLS should not be maybe grouped**: the association
 > between Laggs and TLS is unknown. However, most studies are
 > saying that those are the three maturation stages

> height="2.2054418197725285in"}

ltu:

> [[https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/00220345231151685]{.underline}](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/00220345231151685)

- **TLS prevalence and associations**

 - TLS are present in 50% of patients and 23% of them contain GC+
 > TLSs (Kasikova 2024, Lanickova 2024, MacFawn 2024)

 - TLS formation in HGSC is lower than in other solid cancers

 - most HGSC have TLS, with bona fide (=mature GC+) TLS in about
 > 23% of patient tumors

 - GC+ TLSs more likely in omentum and fallopian tube than ovarian
 > tumors

 - CAFs: negate prognostic effects of TLSs, reduce B cell
 > recruitment and function

 - CAFs associated with Laggs (rather than GC+ TLS) (Xu 2024)

 - NACT-treated tumors have more mature TLSs than NACT-naïve

 - High T and B cell number in the tumor, especially when located
 > in TLSs is beneficial for survival

 - Metastases were more immune-rich (B, CD8, TLS). Also NACT
 > enriched immune features in metastases more than in primary
 > tumors (Lanickova 2024)

 - Fewer and smaller lymphoid aggregates were detected in early
 > relapse patients (Xu 2024)

 - Prognostic value of TLS depends on cancer type, cellular
 > composition, and presence of regulatory cells

 - In some cancers (e.g., ccRCC, HCC), high TLS density
 > correlates with poor prognosis, possibly due to
 > pro-tumorigenic cytokine environments.

 - TLS form in response to chronic inflammation. In autoimmune
 > diseases and age-related chronic inflammation (=where immune
 > system attack self-tissues) TLS usually associated with poorer
 > prognosis // in cancer and infectious diseases (=conditions
 > requiring enhanced immune response) TLS are associated with
 > more favourable outcomes (Zhao 2024)

 - Intratumoral TLS often associated with better prognosis. The

 - TLS in tumor core or invasive margin may be sites of the

- **Milky spots**

 - Develop during faetal development (innate milky spots) or
 > postnatally in response to irritation (for instance infection,
 > obesity, inflammation, cancer metastasis) (Okabe)

 - In human, Omentum harbours more than 100 milky spots with
 > diameters ranging from 300 to 700 μm (Okabe 2024)

 - FALC = fat-associated lymphoid cluster. Covers all lymphoid
 > structures that develop to adipose tissues (in omentum,

 - Preliminary classification of TLS into 9 classes based on
 > location in TME and maturation

 - A1) Lagg = immune cell cluster

 - A2) Immature TLS = organized cluster with a visible GC-like
 > structure

 - A3) Mature TLS = organized cluster with a clearly visible GC

 - B1) Tumor = located in the intratumorl)

 - B2) Stroma = located in the stromal area

 - B3) Interface = located at the boundary between tumor and
 > stroma

 - Arbitrary classification, some immature TLS might be
 > mature TLS, some Lagg might be immature TLS, some
 > scattered and small Lagg might have been missed

 - Also the localization not always clear because of the
 > scattered tumor nests

 > level

 > to do before cell segmentation and cycif are ready

bution within TME

- **Possible markers for CycIF**

 - **CD3:** T cells 🡪 CD8: cytotoxic, CD4: helper

 - scattered in milky spots (not found in all milky spots) and
 > Lagg

 - zoning in immature (distinct zone) and mature (zone around
 > GC) TLS

 - CD3 and CD4 markers are weak and CD8 is strong 🡪 test to mix
 > all antibodies

 - find suitable dilutions \> ½ dilution from CD8?

 - all three antibodies should be from the same species and
 > isotype (IgG)

 - **CD20:** B cells

 - in most Laggs and milky spots, scattered

 - found in immature (zones), and mature (around GC) TLS

 - only CD20+ Lagg potentially can develop into mature TLS

 - **Ki-67:** Proliferating cells

 - possible expression in milky spots and Lagg

 - higher but scattered in immature TLS

 - typically high and localized to GCs in mature TLS

 - ki-67 expression in GC refer to active GC/TLS

 - **CD21:** Follicular dendritic cells (FDCs)

 - immature TLS and mTLS (in GC)

 - can be some level, scattered expression also in Lagg

 - typically absent in milky spots

 - identification of GCs (follicular structures:
 > architecture/skeleton), also possibly differentiating
 > milky spots from Lagg

 - **Some articles state that iTLS do not have CD21+FDCs (for
 > example Sun 2024 rw). Still CD23+ should be defining
 > marker for mTLS**

 - **PNAd:** high endothelial venules (HEVs)

 - HEVs are specialized immune cells trafficking immune cells
 > into lymph nodes

 - strongest expression in mature TLS (all around TLS) where
 > HEVs are fully developed

 - possible weak expression also in immature TLS

 - typically absent in Lagg but present in milky spots

 - differentiating Lagg from immature and mature TLS

 - **IBA-1:** macrophages

 - always in milky spots

aktiivinen

> \#######################

Werner 2021 luokittelu:

1\) CD20 \> B-cells

2\) CD4 \> T helpers

3\) CXCL13 \> B cell attracting chemokine

4\) CD21 \> immature FDCs

5\) CD23 \> mature FDCs

6\) BCL6 \> chemokine of GC referring to mature TLS

\>\>\> Lagg = CD20+, (CXCL13+/-), (scattered CD4+/-), CD21-, CD23-,
BCL6-

\>\>\> iTLS = CD20+, CD4+, CD21+, CD23-, BCL6-

\>\>\> mTLS = CD20+, CD4+, CD21+, CD23+, BCL6+/-

\##########################

PANEL OPTIONS

A\) Precise TLS classification, poor milky spot detection

\- CD4+CD8 \> T cells

\- CD20 \> B cells

\- CD21 \> immature FDCs

\- CD23 \> mature FDCs

TLS structures:

1\) milky spot/Lagg: CD20+, (scattered CD3+/-), CD21-, CD23-

2\) immature TLS: CD20+, CD3+, CD21+, CD23-

3\) mature TLS: CD20+, CD3+, CD21+, CD23+

B\) Not precise differentiation of iTLS/mTLS, better Lagg/milky spot
differentiation

1\) CD4+CD8 \> T cells

2\) CD20 \> B cells

3\) CD21 \> FDCs

4\) IBA-1 \> macrophages

TLS structures:

1\) milky spots: CD20+, (scattered CD3+/-), CD68+, CD21-

2\) Lagg: CD20+, (scattered CD3+/-), (scattered CD68+/-), CD21-

3\) immature and mature TLS: CD20+, CD3+, (scattered CD68+/-), CD21+

TLS project meeting

Today\'s meeting notes:

1\. TLS Segmentation Strategy

- Mesmer vs. Stardist:

 - Mesmer shows better performance with longer running times
 > compared to Stardist.

 > Mesmer runs on small cropped tiles; additional resources are
 > needed to apply it to full-slide images.

 > Tile stitching consideration: ensure proper handling of cells
 > in overlapping regions.

 > Black areas (bubbles) can cause segmentation error -- better
 > skipping and need to filter or avoid black tiles.

 > Benefits of Mesmer:

 - Performs more accurately on thicker tissue sections.

 > This improved performance will benefit future projects,
 > including proteopicks using thicker sections.

 > Optimization Plan:

 - Ziqi will spend one week post-Easter to optimize and upgrade the
 > Mesmer-based segmentation pipeline. Lina has scripts that
 > could support this process.

 > Backup Plan:

 - If whole-slide segmentation is not feasible, fallback will be to
 > crop individual TLS regions and segment them using Mesmer
 > separately.

2\. Tissue Section Thickness Considerations

- 4 μm sections:

 - Used in SPACE project, better but still suboptimal for
 > segmentation of mature TLS structures.

 > 5 μm sections (current study):

ions and segment them using Mesmer

2\. Tissue Section Thickness Considerations

- 4 μm sections:

 - Used in SPACE project, better but still suboptimal for
 > segmentation of mature TLS structures.

 > 5 μm sections (current study):

 - Chosen to collect more material for GeoMx analysis, causing
 > segmentation issues in dense areas like TLSs.

 > General Note:

 - Thinner sections are preferable for H&E staining.

 > Thicker sections are more suitable for RNA/protein profiling.

 > A future reporting system is applied to track section

## Segmentation Test - MESMER

Codes are in Github

Test files are here:
P:\\h30492\\farkkilab2\\7_TLS\\Segmentation_test\\STARDIST_AND_MESMER

Based on Ziqi, Zhihan and Ada's work:

### **1. Image Preprocessing Workflow**

- Multichannel TIFF images were processed using:

 - **Gamma correction** (gamma=γ) to enhance contrast.

 - **Unsharp masking** (sigma=σ, strength=s) to sharpen nuclear
 > features.

- Channels (e.g., DAPI, membrane) were stacked into a 3D array format
 > and reshaped to comply with MESMER's input shape: (1, H, W, C).

### **2. Tiling Strategy**

- Images were divided into overlapping tiles using a fixed tile size
 > and dynamically computed overlaps to ensure full coverage.

- This produced a tile array of shape (B, num_x, num_y, tile_x,
 > tile_y, C).

### **3. Filtering Background Tiles with GMM and Other Metrics**

To optimize nuclear segmentation by avoiding background-only regions,
several strategies were tested for identifying and excluding

y of shape (B, num_x, num_y, tile_x,
 > tile_y, C).

### **3. Filtering Background Tiles with GMM and Other Metrics**

To optimize nuclear segmentation by avoiding background-only regions,
several strategies were tested for identifying and excluding
low-information tiles before running MESMER. These methods aimed to
reduce unnecessary computation and improve segmentation quality.

#### **3.1 Baseline Thresholding with Mean Intensity**

- **Method**: Calculated the **mean pixel intensity** of each tile on
 > the DAPI channel (channel_idx=0).

- **Threshold**: Tiles with mean \< 3500 were excluded from
 > segmentation.

- **Outcome**: Performed well on images with consistent staining but
 > failed in cases with uneven illumination or high signal
 > heterogeneity.

#### **3.2 Comparison with Sum-Based Filtering**

- **Method**: Used np.sum() of DAPI intensity values instead of the
 > mean.

- **Observation**: Performed poorly despite identical tile sizes.
 > Since np.sum scales linearly with total intensity, it was more
 > sensitive to background noise and illumination bias, leading to
 > misclassification of low-content tiles.

- **Conclusion**: np.mean was more robust due to normalization by
 > pixel count.

#### **3.3 Feature Combination: Mean + Standard Deviation**

- **Method**: Combined **mean** and **standard deviation** (std) of
 > DAPI intensities per tile into 2D feature vectors.

- **Goal**: Capture both brightness and texture/variance of tiles.

- **Input to GMM**: X = \[\[mean₁, std₁\], \[mean₂, std₂\], \...,
 > \[meanₙ, stdₙ\]\]

- **Result**: Improved separation of background vs. foreground in
 > heterogeneous images.

#### **3.4 Median Intensity Filtering**

- **Trial**: Replaced mean with **median intensity** as a more
 > outlier-robust statistic.

,
 > \[meanₙ, stdₙ\]\]

- **Result**: Improved separation of background vs. foreground in
 > heterogeneous images.

#### **3.4 Median Intensity Filtering**

- **Trial**: Replaced mean with **median intensity** as a more
 > outlier-robust statistic.

- **Result**: Median has a close result compared to mthe ean.

#### **3.5 Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) Clustering**

- **Method**: Applied sklearn.mixture.GaussianMixture to intensity
 > features (mean or \[mean + std\]) across all tiles.

- **Parameters**:

 - n_components = 3 (assumed: background, low-signal, high-signal)

 - random_state = 0 for reproducibility

- **Label Assignment**:

 - Automatically determined the cluster with the **lowest mean** as
 > background (via
 > filter_tiles_by_mean_intensity_gmm_auto_background)

 - Background labels were excluded from downstream prediction

A Gaussian mixture model (GMM) is a machine learning method used to determine the probability each data point belongs to a given cluster. The model is a soft clustering method used in unsupervised learning.

components = 3

## Slide 3
QC-Part1 SelectROIs in Cylinter
Images were processed with Cylinter 0.5 module selectROIs to remove:
Big detached areas
Bubbles, intensity blobs, hairs etc
Segmented cells around the tissue, eg near the borders, in corners etc
Segmentation artifacts
High autofluorescent regions within blood vessels that have been 
No area/intensity filters will be conducted in cylinter

## Slide 4
2. UNIFORM
UNIFORM is based on the idea of matching distributions. Therefore, to ensure no inference of false positive cells (small masked pixels), we removed the <30px Area cells to calculate the shifts. Then, all data, with no exceptions, was normalized and exported

## Slide 5
2. UNIFORM- Reference sample exporation
UNIFORM requires a reference sample as the model distribution for the algorithm. Results might vary a lot depending on which sample is the reference
Example: Marker normalized with two different reference samples
I iteratively tested every marker with every image, selected the best matching references and checked that the image patterns worked well before constructing the reference dictionnary

## Slide 16
High intensity artifacts
Problem: In working antibodies, scanning-related artifacts are as high intensity as the TLS areas. Example: CD20. This is common to >⅓ of the slides.

## Slide 17
White top-hat filtering
1. Morphological Opening:
A morphological opening is an operation that combines erosion followed by dilation, using a structuring element.
The structuring element acts as a probe that slides over the image, and the erosion removes parts of the image that don't "fit" inside the structuring element. Then, dilation expands the remaining parts back out, but only to the extent allowed by the structuring element's shape and size.
In white top-hat filtering, the opening operation removes larger foreground objects and smooths out the background. 
2. White Top-Hat Calculation:
The white top-hat transform is then calculated by subtracting the opened image from the original image.
This subtraction isolates the bright features (details) in the original image that were removed by the opening operation

Patterns in Tissue Architecture
Investigate distinct patterns or structural changes in tissue linked to TLS presence across diverse clinical and molecular profiles

Comparison of LAgg and TLS Regions
Analyze spatial and functional differences between TLSs and other LAgg-containing samples

Proteomic and Transcriptomic Insights

We aim to understand TLS structures and their development in ovarian cancer
TLSs are enriched in IDS omentum compared to other tissue sites
TLS
Lymphoid aggregates (Lagg)

Role of tumor genetics
Influence of transcriptomics

Could milky spots be part of this process?

Hanna

## Slide 3
Milky spots
>100 MS in human omentum, 300-700 um diameter

induce inflammation but lack DCs → no antigen presenting capability

unlike conventional lymphoid organs, develop in the absence of LTi

primary colonization site for tumor cells in omentum → can be later replaced withtumor mass

Fibroblastic reticular cells (FRC) are specialized lymphoid organ fibroblasts surrounding and supporting MS, Lagg, and TLS (increasing expression MS → Lagg → iTLS → mTLS)

## Slide 4
Milky spots vs. Lagg
Lagg develop in response during inflammation but also MS may activate in some level

less HEVs: ICs in Lagg are recruited via HEVs
lower IL-6 expression
less proliferating cells (Ki-67)
less abundant and less active fibroblastic reticular cell (FRC) network around MS (=lower PDPN expression)
more or less M1/M2 macrophages? M1 count increases in the beginning of inflammation but M2 more prevalent in chronic inflammation and cancer

## Slide 5
Questions from low-plex tCycIF: ben/bord omentum samples vs. malignant PDS omentum samples
Descriptive analysis of MS and Lagg
Can we distinguish MS from Lagg from PDS omentum cases? diameter, cell density, cell composition (CD4/CD8, M1/M2, PD-1, Ki-67)
How many “Lagg” in malignant samples are actually MS
Are some ben/bord aggregates Lagg rather than MS

BCR profiling - SCellex
Main aims:
Verify the feasibility of the SCellex platform for BCR (and optional TCR) on our samples in FF and FFPE. 
STEPS:

whether SCellex's BCR/TCR amplification can work (gene-specific / random amplification)

FF samples for selected samples

## Slide 16
BCR profiling - MiXCR for Bulk RNAseq
Main aims:
The BCR sequence was extracted and reconstructed from the existing Bulk RNA-seq data on the iCAN platform using the MiXCR software to identify clonal and immune diversity, and compared with morphology and expression data (B cell enrichment level, TLS group, etc.).
Compare MiXCR results with deconvolution results from Kassandra & PRISM. 
STEPS:

diversity, and compared with morphology and expression data (B cell enrichment level, TLS group, etc.).

QC for MiXCR results
Use the R package immunarch for downstream visualization and statistical analysis
Compare with deconvolution results

GeoMx - Biological questions and next steps
Main questions:
TLS functionality
Is the emergence/maturation of TLS associated with antitumor immunity?
Do the distributions of immunosuppressive markers and activation markers differ in TLS at different maturity levels?
TLS maturation axis
How can the continuous spectrum of TLS from basic to mature be quantified?
Recruitment / positioning mechanism of TLS
How are TLS B/T cell recruitment and spatial positioning driven by chemokine?
Cell interactions and spatial mechanisms of TLS
How do B/T cell colocalization patterns change with TLS maturity?
Do specific cell-cell interaction patterns exist?
Clonality and function
TLS in TME
Does TLS drive the distribution or functional state of immune cells in the tumor microenvironment?
Treatment relevance & predictive markers

## Slide 17
GeoMx - Biological questions and next steps
Core objective: To understand the biological functions, molecular characteristics, and spatial organization of TLS at different maturation stages (lagg → iTLS → mTLS), while exploring heterogeneity within TLS and between patients.
Main questions:
Defining TLS Maturation Trajectories (LAgg → iTLS → mTLS) and Validating pseudo-time inference
Can pseudo-time trajectories capture the maturation gradient of TLS?
Which genes or pathways show linear or non-linear changes across pseudo-time？
How can we delineate early/intermediate/late TLS stages based on transcriptomic signatures?

inference
Can pseudo-time trajectories capture the maturation gradient of TLS?
Which genes or pathways show linear or non-linear changes across pseudo-time？
How can we delineate early/intermediate/late TLS stages based on transcriptomic signatures?
Which canonical chemokines best distinguish TLS maturation stages? Are chemokine gradients spatially aligned with pseudo-time ordering?

What are the molecular dynamics of TLS structural formation and functional maturation?
Identify the sequential molecular programs driving TLS maturation
Do key programs such as B cell proliferation, SHM/CSR, terminal differentiation, and Tfh helper signaling initiate in the expected order?
What are the temporal dynamics of different functional modules during TLS/GC maturation? Are there any coordination or asynchrony between modules?
Does TB maturation precede or follow GC maturation, and what might that imply for TLS functionality?

## Slide 18
GeoMx - Biological questions and next steps
Main questions:
Intra- and Inter-Patient Heterogeneity of TLS
Intra-patient heterogeneity
Are TLS maturation levels consistent within a single patient, or do multiple maturation states coexist?
Does TLS location (intra-tumoral vs. stromal/peritumoral) affect maturation level or composition?
Is TLS heterogeneity correlated with patients (HRD/HRP, BRCA status, immune infiltration pattern)?

Clinical Relevance and Predictive Potential
Are there stage-specific markers predictive of treatment response or resistance?

Spatial Ecosystem Integration
How does TLS maturation relate to the composition and spatial organization of the surrounding tumor microenvironment (e.g., myeloid or stromal niches)?
Are there specific cell–cell interaction motifs that emerge or disappear with TLS maturation?

## Slide 2
The binary classification of TLSs into immature TLSs (iTLSs)/early TLSs (eTLSs) and mature TLSs (mTLSs)
1) early TLSs (eTLSs), in which lymphocytes aggregate diffusely and CD21+ cells are scarce, and 2) follicle-formed mature TLSs, in which the follicular morphology of SLOs and CD21+ follicular DCs (FDCs) are distributed in a reticular pattern (17). Mature TLSs (mTLSs) with GCs and DCs reportedly represent privileged sites for local antigen presentation and contribute to the generation of tumor-targeting effector T cells and B cell

## Slide 3
Nielsen et al. discovered that typical markers of antigen-presenting cells, such as MHC class I/II, CD40, CD80, and CD86, were expressed on TLS-associated B cells in HGSOC. As these TLS-associated B cells are disconnected from serum autoantibodies and colocalize with CD8+ T cells, Nielsen et al. suggested that instead of producing antibodies, the predominant function of TLS-associated B cells in HGSOC is to present antigens to CD8+ T cells

## Slide 6
SPACEstat: Defining TLS
Size/cell count-based classification
TLS: immature TLS (20-300 nuclei), intermediate TLS (300-500 nuclei), mature TLS (500+ nuclei) (MacFawn 2024)

Cell count, type and proximity-based classification
Lagg: epithelial enrichment score <0.8, combined T and B cell enrichment scores >1, clusters must include >50 cells, cells must be within 15 µm of each other (Xu 2024)

Cell count, type and zoning (Kroeger 2016)
type I: 20-50 cells, T and B cells, occasionally DCs > “small Lagg” 
type II: 100+ cells, T and B cells, lacked discrete zones or follicles > “large Lagg”
type III: B cell follicles with GC-like structures, discrete T cell zone, DCs and HEV-like vessels > “mature TLS”
type IV: 100-300 B cells, DC follicles, few T cells > as primarily found in omentum, these were speculated to be mostly normal milky spots

Milky spots: 300-750 µm diameter (Liu 2016, Okabe 2024)

**Histopathological Characterization of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures
across Ovarian Cancer Histotypes and Molecular profiles in Ovarian
Cancer.**

**Authors:** Hanna Elomaa^1^, Ada Junquera^1^, Zhihan Liang^1^, Matilda
Salko^1^, Oskari Lehtonen^1^, Joona Sarkkinen^2^, Anni Virtanen^1,3^,
Anniina Färkkilä^1,4,5^

**Introduction**

Tertiary lymphoid structures (TLSs) are ectopic immune formations
associated with anti-tumor immunity, favorable prognosis, and improved
immunotherapy responses in several solid tumors. However, the
distribution and function of TLSs across ovarian cancer histologies are
poorly characterized. TLSs display a maturation spectrum, from
disorganized lymphoid aggregates to highly organized structures with
elevated immunological activity. In this study, we systematically
assessed the prevalence, maturation, and clinical relevance of TLSs, and
examined their associations with molecular features and treatment
strategies to clarify their role across ovarian cancer histotypes.

**Material and method**

Hematoxylin and eosin-stained tumor sections from a prospective cohort
of 283 ovarian cancer patients were analyzed to characterize TLSs using
a deep learning-based segmentation pipeline. Tumor mutational and gene
expression profiles were analysed using whole-exome sequencing,
homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) testing and deconvoluted bulk
RNA sequencing. Using multi-modal spatial data, TLSs were classified
into lymphoid aggregates, immature TLSs, and mature TLSs. The spatial
localization and size of TLSs were recorded, and they were
computationally segmented for quantification of cellular composition and
structure. TLS patterns were compared across histotypes, and associated
with the molecular and clinical data of the patients, including

ize of TLSs were recorded, and they were
computationally segmented for quantification of cellular composition and
structure. TLS patterns were compared across histotypes, and associated
with the molecular and clinical data of the patients, including
responses to chemotherapy and overall survival.

**Results and discussion**

The most common histotypes were omental (65%), adnexal (19%), and
ovarian (11%) tumors, reflecting typical primary and metastatic site
distribution. We observed marked heterogeneity in the presence, number,
and localization of TLSs. TLSs were detected in 49 percent of tumors,
most commonly as lymphoid aggregates and predominantly enriched in the
omentum. Immature or mature TLSs were found in 22 percent of tumors and
were exclusively located in the omentum. Their absence in other sites
suggests that TLS maturation could be influenced by the local tissue
context, with the omentum potentially providing favorable stromal or
immunological conditions for their development. Given that mature TLSs
have been linked to improved survival and better responses to
immunotherapy in other solid tumors, their presence in ovarian cancer
may hold prognostic and therapeutic relevance. Linking TLS features with
genomic and expression profiles may reveal drivers of TLS development
and clarify their significance.

**Conclusion**\
Our findings highlight the importance of histotypes and molecular
profiles in shaping anti-tumor immune responses and should be considered
when evaluating TLSs as prognostic or therapeutic targets in ovarian
cancer histotypes.

*A Spatially Resolved Multi-Omics Atlas of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures
and Tumor-Infiltrating Lymphocytes in Ovarian Cancer*

Authors: Zhihan Liang^1^, Ada Junquera^1^, Hanna Elomaa^1^, Lina
Maltrovsky^1^, Joona Sarkkinen^2^, Iga Niemiec^1^, Aleksandra
Shabanova^1^, Anni Virtanen^1,4^, Matilda Salko^1^, Anniina
Färkkilä^1,3,5,6^

1 Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki,
Helsinki, Finland

2 Translational Immunology Research Program, University of Helsinki and
Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland

3 Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Department of Oncology,
Clinical Trials Unit, Comprehensive Cancer Center, Helsinki University
Hospital, Helsinki, Finland

4 Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic
Center, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland

5 iCAN Digital Precision Cancer Medicine Flagship, Helsinki, Finland

6 Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute for Life
Sciences, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland

**Introduction**

Tertiary lymphoid structures (TLSs) and tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes
(TILs) are key components of the tumor microenvironment, facilitating
local anti-tumor immune responses. TLSs presence has been linked to
improved outcomes in ovarian cancer, yet their spatial organization and
molecular characteristics remain underexplored. We constructed a
spatially resolved multi-omics atlas by integrating histopathology,
transcriptomics, proteomics, and multiplexed imaging with genomic and
clinical data (homologous recombination status, PDS/IDS) to characterize
TLSs and TILs features, aiming to reveal their clinical associations and
underlying mechanisms in ovarian cancer.

**Material and method**

multiplexed imaging with genomic and
clinical data (homologous recombination status, PDS/IDS) to characterize
TLSs and TILs features, aiming to reveal their clinical associations and
underlying mechanisms in ovarian cancer.

**Material and method**

Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) sections from over 200 Oncosys-Ova samples
were annotated to identify immune aggregates, immature TLSs, and mature
TLSs with germinal centers (GCs).

From these, 45 formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) high-grade serous
ovarian cancer (HGSC) omentum samples were selected for detailed spatial
single-cell, transcriptomic and proteomic profiling. All samples were
collected post-neoadjuvant chemotherapy during interval debulking
surgery, accompanied by clinical and WES + bulk RNA sequencing data.
t-CyCIF imaging was performed using a 30-marker immune antibody panel,
and adjacent sections underwent spatial transcriptomic profiling by
GeoMx, focusing on selected regions of interest (ROIs) within TLSs and
TILs. Proteomic profiling was conducted via proteo-picking, a novel
microtissue extraction method, followed by mass spectrometry to identify
proteins across TLS compartments.

**Result and discussion**

Our analysis revealed heterogeneity in TLSs and TILs features across
ovarian cancer samples. Using an algorithm-assisted approach, H&E
annotation identified distinct immune aggregate patterns, with TLSs
maturity varying between patients. Notably, patients with poor prognosis
and homologous recombination proficiency lacked GCs, despite numerous
immune aggregates. Spatial metrics, including TLSs proximity to tumor,
were calculated to assess TLSs distribution and functionality across
molecular subtypes and clinical outcomes.

Processed t-CycIF images were analyzed using SCIMAP and UTAG to uncover

e aggregates. Spatial metrics, including TLSs proximity to tumor,
were calculated to assess TLSs distribution and functionality across
molecular subtypes and clinical outcomes.

Processed t-CycIF images were analyzed using SCIMAP and UTAG to uncover
functional immune composition within TLSs/TILs. Based on these results,
we selected 3 ROIs per TLSs on the adjacent sections: GCs, surrounding
T-B cells, and stromal areas, alongside another 5 ROIs per sample
representing immune aggregates.

In total, 268 ROIs were profiled by GeoMx to obtain spatial gene
expression data from TLSs/TILs compartments. These ROIs also guided
spatially matched proteomic analysis and achieved comprehensive
multi-omics data integration.

**Conclusion**

Our spatially resolved multi-omics atlas reveals patient-specific
heterogeneity in TLS maturity, spatial organization, and immune
composition, uncovering mechanistic insights into immune activation
linked to clinical outcomes.

2498/2500 characters

**Material and Methods**

HE

**tCycIF**

Samples were stained consecutively using a panel of validated antibodies
(IDS samples: Table XX, PDS and milky-spots samples: Table XX ) and
scanned on a RareCyte CyteFinder system according to the standard
t-CyCIF protocol (CITE) at a resolution of 0.65 um pixel with 2x2
binning. Raw image files underwent illumination correction using the
BaSiC tool. Stitching and registration were performed utilizing ASHLAR
(v1.17.0) (CITE) to generate unified, multi-channel OME-TIFF images.
Nuclear segmentation was performed using Mesmer (CITE), a deep
learning-based segmentation algorithm.

To address low signal-to-noise ratios (SNR) and mitigate tissue
autofluorescence, a morphological white top-hat filter was applied to a
subset of the lineage marker channels (Table XX) across all images. Mean
fluorescence intensities for individual cells were quantified from the
filtered images using Python's scikit-image library via custom in-house
scripts:
[[https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing)
. Fluorescence intensities were log-transformed and normalized through
the UNIFORM Python package (CITE).

Single-cell phenotyping was performed hierarchically and iteratively
using TRIBUS (CITE), which annotates cells semi-automatically and
iteratively through SOM- based clustering. Single-cell feature
quantification table and cell phenotype marker positivity and negativity
were used as inputs. Semi-automated labels were further curated through
manual gates of the markers used for cell type characterization (Table
XX).

Recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCNs) were computed using the Scimap
Python package. Neighborhood matrixes were computed using

tomated labels were further curated through
manual gates of the markers used for cell type characterization (Table
XX).

Recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCNs) were computed using the Scimap
Python package. Neighborhood matrixes were computed using
tl.spatial_count with a r=100px (\~65um), and the resulting neighborhood
matrix was then clustered using tl.spatial_cluster using kmeans
clustering with k=8.

#6 EF cells?
EF cells are fighting a dense matrix and they look exhausted (DUSP1, SOCS3)

Main results so far (20-02-2026)
0- tCycIF: we observe EF Bcells s in tCycIF, near tumor and surrounded by stroma and other immune cells. Average distances from B cells to tumor and stroma cells are significantly shorter from EF than from TLS sites.

Main results so far (20-02-2026)
0- tCycIF: we observe EF Bcells s in tCycIF, near tumor and surrounded by stroma and other immune cells. Average distances from B cells to tumor and stroma cells are significantly shorter from EF than from TLS sites. Distances to other B cells average 20um, while in EF the average distance triplicates (60um), suggestive for looser distributions and increased heterotypic interactions.

Regarding functional states, few samples contain >5% Ki67+ of their EF B cells with higher BCL6+ frequency ( 5-15%) - I THINK THIS IS A TECHNICAL ARTIFACT FROM BCL6 stromal autofluorescence. BCL6+ and Ki67+ status of the TLSs (Averaged by sample) seems to correlate nicely with the EF functional status. EF B cell counts correlates with TLS Bcell counts as defined by DBSCAN density-based clustering. Both the functional status and count correlation with B cells in TLS suggest EF cells might be sourced out of the TLSs. 

Xenium: EF, TLS and LAgg areas were annotated based on tCycIF CD20+ stainings and Xenium morphology stainings, obtaining 182 regions from 5 patients.
 
1- Composition : EF B cell-containing areas present a smaller b cell fraction than LAgg and mature TLSs. Also lower CD8Tcell and myeloid content. As expected, EF sites present higher fractions of Fibroblasts and Tumor (explained by their spatial location), as well as higher % of plasma cells. EF sites are heterogeneous and display distinct phenotypic composition

2. DGE of phenotypes across TLS and EF.

EF B cells display high expression of various immunoglobulin heavy and light chains (IGKC, IGHG4, IGHG3, IGHGP). This indicates that EF B cells are likely transitioning toward an antibody-secreting effector state. DUSP1 suggests these cells are responding to environmental stressors

of various immunoglobulin heavy and light chains (IGKC, IGHG4, IGHG3, IGHGP). This indicates that EF B cells are likely transitioning toward an antibody-secreting effector state. DUSP1 suggests these cells are responding to environmental stressors

EF macrophages appear highly active and focused on tissue interaction and signaling. upregulation of FCGR3A (CD16) and FCGR2A suggests these macrophages are primed for antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC) or phagocytosis of immune complexes. Interestingly CD163 is upreg in EF sites.

The EF plasma cells seem to be in a state of high protein synthesis but also potentially under regulatory control.SOCS3, a potent inhibitor of JAK/STAT signaling is upregulated. This might indicate a negative feedback loop to limit over-activation in the EF space or a response to specific local cytokines (like IL-6).

## Page 1
 
Nano -Pick: A Novel Method Enables Spatially 
Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary 
Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer 
 
 
Translational Medicine (TRANSMED) 
Study Track: Cancer 
Master's thesis 
 
 
Author: 
Zhihan Liang 
 
 
Supervisor(s): 
Anniina Fä rkkilä 
Ada Junquera Mencí a 
 
 
29.04.2024 
Helsinki 
 

## Page 2
2 Abstract 
High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) is the most aggressive subtype of ovarian 
cancer. Most patients are diagnosed at an advanced stage, resulting in a poor 
prognosis. Although some targeted therapies have made good progress, immune 
checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) still show limited efficacy in HGSC treatment. This is 
closely associated with the high heterogeneity and unique immunosuppressive tumor 
microenvironment (TME) of HGSC. As local immune centers in the TME, tertiary 
lymphoid structures (TLSs) contain diverse types of immune cells and play a crucial 
role in the anti-tumor immune process. Nonetheless, the spatial immune landscape of 
TLSs and key gene expression signatures affecting prognosis have not been fully 
elucidated. In recent years, spatially resolved gene expression profiling of clinical 
samples has provided insights into the transcriptome at high resolution. However, 
despite revealing a series of response mechanisms of anti-tumor immunity , these 
methods still have many limitations in non-single cell resolution, specific tissue 
sample types, and high cost. 
Here in this thesis, I establish ed Nano-Pick, a novel method based on precise 
microtissue extraction and the nCounter analysis system. Nano-Pick enables cost-
effective and rapid spatial gene expression profiling using formalin-fixed paraffin-
embedded (FFPE) samples. All TLSs-enriched FFPE slides were obtained from

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
precise 
microtissue extraction and the nCounter analysis system. Nano-Pick enables cost-
effective and rapid spatial gene expression profiling using formalin-fixed paraffin-
embedded (FFPE) samples. All TLSs-enriched FFPE slides were obtained from 
patient-derived HGSC samples at Helsinki University Hospital (HUH). With the help 
of tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CycIF), a highly multiplexed imaging method, 
selected regions of interests (ROIs) were annotated in different zones of TLSs. These 
ROIs were accurately transferred to adjacent tissue sections, guiding the target region 
selection for microtissue extraction. An improved immunofluorescence staining 
method was used to successfully label cancer cells, macrophages, and immune cells 
without antigen retrieval. With the optimized combination of parameters, efficient and 
consistent precise picking for different cell types was achieved. mRNA extracted from 
the collected microtissues was validated by qPCR for the range of pre-amplification 
cycles, confirming its suitability for nCounter gene panel analysis. Therefore, Nano-
Pick offers the opportunity for higher-resolution spatial analysis and interactions of 
immune cell populations in the TME, especially in the TLSs. This low-cost, reliable 
and sensitive spatial gene expression profiling method has broad application prospects 
in many fields, with the potential to advance the development of precision medicine.

## Page 5
 
5 
 1 Literature review 
1.1 Ovarian cancer 
Ovarian cancer is the most lethal type of gynecological malignant tumor. Globally, 
more than 310,000 new cases of ovarian cancer are diagnosed per year, leading to the 
death of over 200,000 women (WCRF International, 2020). Due to the difficulty of

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0005
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
arian cancer is the most lethal type of gynecological malignant tumor. Globally, 
more than 310,000 new cases of ovarian cancer are diagnosed per year, leading to the 
death of over 200,000 women (WCRF International, 2020). Due to the difficulty of 
early diagnosis and high invasiveness, only 30% of ovarian cancer patients survive 
more than 5 years after first diagnosis (Baldwin et al., 2012). 
Ovarian cancer is a highly heterogeneous disease. It can be categorized into several 
histological subtypes based on diverse clinicopathological and molecular features. 
Epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC) is the most predominant type, which encompasses a 
range of specific subtypes including high -grade serous carcinoma (HGSC), low -grade 
serous carcinoma (LGSC), endometrioid carcinoma, clear cell carcinoma, and 
mucinous carcinoma (Kaku et al., 2003). EOC is believed to originate from abnormal 
epithelial cell proliferation in fallopian tubes (Labidi -Galy et al., 2017), while other 
subtypes of non -epithelial ovarian cancer (NEOC) arise from germ -cell, sex-cord, or 
stromal tissue (Kim et al., 2018). 
1.1.1 HGSC 
As the most common and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer, HGSC has a notably 
poor prognosis, accounting for approximately 70% of all ovarian cancer deaths (Peres 
et al., 2019). This is due to the advanced stage at which it is often diagnosed, as well 
as its rapid development and aggressive nature (Dao et al., 2016). Around 75% of 
HGSC patients are diagnosed at stage III or IV according to the FIGO staging system 
(Peres et al., 2019) , indicating that tumor cells have spread to distant lymph nodes and 
other tissues within the pelvi s (stage III), or distant metastasis are present (stage IV) .

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0006
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
agnosed at stage III or IV according to the FIGO staging system 
(Peres et al., 2019) , indicating that tumor cells have spread to distant lymph nodes and 
other tissues within the pelvi s (stage III), or distant metastasis are present (stage IV) . 
The new dualistic model of ovarian carcinogenesis categorizes HGSC as a type II 
carcinoma with high -grade histology and high proliferative activity , while other 

## Page 6
 
6 
 subtypes that develop from benign extra ovarian lesions are classified as type I 
carcinoma (Kurman & Shih, 2016). 
A key feature of HGSC is its high degree of genetic instability: more than 96% of 
cases harbor TP53 mutations (Kuhn et al., 2012 ). Also, genetic mutations in the 
homologous recombination repair (HRR) pathway are very common, such as 
alterations in BRCA1 or BRCA2 (Perets et al., 2013). HGSC is heavily dependent on 
the HRR to repair DNA double -strand breaks (DSBs) from exogenous anticancer 
treatments such as ionizing radiation. Compared with the non -homologous end -
joining (NHEJ) pathway, HRR uses homologous D NA templates to assist the repair, 
which significantly reduces the error rate (Ledermann et al., 2016). However, because 
of related genes’ copy number variation, around 50% of HGSC patients exhibit an 
HRR deficiency (HRD)(Fuh et al., 2020) . These HRD patients are more sensitive to 
platinum-based chemotherapy because they cannot repair DNA damage induced by 
chemotherapy drugs . HRD patients also benefit more from the synthetic lethality 
approach provided by PARP inhibitors (PARPi), although the molecular mechanisms 
affecting their susceptibility or resistance remain a subject of ongoing study 
(Konstantinopoulos et al., 2015). 
1.1.2 Clinical treatments

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0007
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
m the synthetic lethality 
approach provided by PARP inhibitors (PARPi), although the molecular mechanisms 
affecting their susceptibility or resistance remain a subject of ongoing study 
(Konstantinopoulos et al., 2015). 
1.1.2 Clinical treatments 
The median age for diagnosis of o varian cancer is 63 , with patients usually ranging 
from 55-74 years old (NIH, 2023). Given the invasive nature and high dissemination 
rate of HGSC , cytoreduction surgery is the preferred treatment . Primary debulking 
surgery (PDS) is typically performed for patients at earlier FIGO stages (I or II) , 
where tumor tissues are confined solely to the pelvic cavity and are completely 
removable . The objective of PDS is to eliminate as much visible tumor tissue as 
possible to achieve complete resection. A postoperative finding of tiny residual 
tumors, smaller than 1cm in diameter (R=0 or R<1), is classified as "optimal 
debulking", whereas the discovery of larger residual tumors (R>1) signifies 
"suboptimal debulking" (Hoskins et al., 1994). After surgery, patients often receive 

## Page 7
 
7 
 adjuvant chemotherapy to further eradicate tiny tumor cells that cannot be removed by 
surgery, thereby reducing the risk of relapse and prolonging survival (Matulonis et al., 
2016). Typical chemotherapy regimens involve a combination of platinum -based 
compounds and taxanes , such as carboplatin and paclitaxel (Du Bois et al., 2003). A 
standard course of chemotherapy consists of 6 cycles, which can be adjusted based on 
the patient’s condition and their response to the treatment. If severe side effects occur 
or the cancer tissue shows resistance to the initial medication, the chemotherapy 
regimen must be adjusted or paused. 
For patients diagnosed with advanced stage (e.g.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0008
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
nt’s condition and their response to the treatment. If severe side effects occur 
or the cancer tissue shows resistance to the initial medication, the chemotherapy 
regimen must be adjusted or paused. 
For patients diagnosed with advanced stage (e.g. stage IV), optimal cytoreduction is 
difficult to achieve and neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) is recommended to 
reduce tumor burden before surgery (Gill et al., 2017). NACT typically consists of 3 
cycles of chemotherapy on the combination of platinum and taxanes, but some 
patients also receive NACT on an individualized basis ranging from 1 to 8 cycles 
(Kobal et al., 2018). The approach of neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by interval 
debulking surgery (NACT -IDS) is gradu ally gaining acceptance in clinical settings, 
although its effectiveness compared to direct PDS remains a topic of debate. Results 
from several randomized trials indicate that patients at advanced stages undergoing 
NACT-IDS have no significant differences in progression -free survival (PFS) and 
overall survival (OS) compared to those undergoing PDS directly, but adverse effects 
(such as hemorrhage, venous thromboembolism, infection) and mortality are 
significantly reduced (van der Burg et al., 1995; Rose et al., 2004; Vergote et al., 
2010; Qin et al., 2018). On the contrary , some authors claim that in their clinical trials 
enrolling stage IV ovarian cancer patients, NACT -IDS leads to significantly longer 
OS and PFS (Van Meurs et al., 2013; Vergote et al., 2018). In conclusion , many 
studies support that NACT -IDS shows improvements over PDS, especially for 
advanced HGSC patients with a high tumor burden (Kehoe et al., 2015; Gill et al., 
2017; Liu et al., 2020). 

## Page 8
 
8
