t. TLS presence is associated with a favorable prognosis across various 
cancer types, irrespective of the patient’s tumor stage (Sautès -Fridman et al., 2019). 
Functioning as a critical site within TME, TLS s play a pivotal role in initiating and 
maintaining local and systemic adaptive immune responses against tumors (Dieu‐
Nosjean et al., 2016 ). Clinical research in several different tumor types has confirmed 

## Page 26
 
26 
 that the presence of TLS is associated with the effectiveness of ICI and better survival 
outcomes (Petitprez et al., 2020; Cabrita et al., 2020; Helmink et al., 2020;). In 
HGSOC, recent evidence points to a correlation between the presence of TLS and 
improved patient outcomes, including longer overall survival (OS) and progression -
free survival (PFS) (Zhang et al., 2023; Lu et al., 2023). 
The density, maturation status, and spatial location of TLS s are key factors 
influencing prognostic values in cancer treatment. Several authors confirmed that 
higher TLS s density in the TME is associated with better prognosis in most tumors 
(Sautès-Fridman et al., 2016). Different mature statuses of TLSs lead to distinct 
cellular compositions as well as spatial cell -cell interactions, which are highly 
correlated with their effects on antitumor immune response and prognosis (Zhang & 
Wu, 2023). Previous studi es have shown that HCC and CRC patients with more 
mature TLS s differentiation have a lower risk of recurrence compared with patients 
with only lymphoid aggregates (Posch et al., 2018; Calderaro et al., 2019). 
Differences in TLS s location can also affect the prognosis of cancer patients. 
Compared with HCC patients with intratumoral TLSs, those with only peritumoral

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0037
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
h patients 
with only lymphoid aggregates (Posch et al., 2018; Calderaro et al., 2019). 
Differences in TLS s location can also affect the prognosis of cancer patients. 
Compared with HCC patients with intratumoral TLSs, those with only peritumoral 
TLSs have a significantly higher risk of recurrence (Calderaro et al., 2019). 
Differential gene expression, cellular interactions, and spatial localization in TLS s 
also hold distinct prognostic implications. It is indicated that the presence of CD8+ 
TILs in TLS s alone cannot predict the prognostic effect, but its co -presence with 
plasma cells, CD20+ TILs, and CD4+ TILs is highly associated with positive anti -
tumor immune effects, suggesting synergy among these lymphocyte subpopulations in 
TLSs (Kroeger et al., 2016). Additionally, unique gene expression signatures of TLS -
related plas ma cells in ovarian cancer also show positive prognostic value (Kroeger et 
al., 2016). Another research used a mouse model to demonstrate that tumor -specific B 
and Tfh cells can cooperate to promote anti -tumor CD8+ T cell immune response (Cui 
et al., 2021). Analysis of genes involved in TLS s in the database revealed the 
prognostic value of some gene signatures such as CCL19, CXCL10, and CXCL13 for 

## Page 27
 
27 
 ovarian cancer, primarily by increasing immune infiltration and restricting immune 
escape (Hou et al., 2023). 
The generation and maturation of TLS can be induced through various methods, 
offering the potential to bolster anti -tumor immune responses. In mouse models, 
tissue-specific expression of some cytokines, such as lymphotoxin, TNF α, and 
CXCL13 can trigger TLS development locally (Schumacher & Thommen, 2022). 
ICIs, vaccines, and neoadjuvant chemotherapy have also been reported to increase the

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0038
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
e models, 
tissue-specific expression of some cytokines, such as lymphotoxin, TNF α, and 
CXCL13 can trigger TLS development locally (Schumacher & Thommen, 2022). 
ICIs, vaccines, and neoadjuvant chemotherapy have also been reported to increase the 
number of TLS s in TME (van Dijk et al., 2020; Maldonado et al., 2014; Remark et al., 
2016). However, it's crucial to note that while enhancing TLS function may curb 
tumor progression, it could also heighten autoimmune responses and worsen 
autoimmune toxicity (Schumacher & Thommen, 2022). Further clinical trials are 
necessary to validate the safety of such approaches and thoroughly assess associated 
risks with TLS induction. 
Importantly, the immune cell composition of TLS s exhibits noticeable heterogeneity 
across different cancer types and among individual patients (Sautès -Fridman et al., 
2019). This variability highlights the significance of developing and implementing 
clinical precision medicine approaches tailored to the unique im mune landscape of 
each patient. 
1.4 Methods enabling the spatial exploration of the TME 
Visualizing and characterizing the spatial interactions between immune cells and 
tumor cells in the cancer TME is crucial for discovering new prognostic markers and 
therapeutic targets. Traditional techniques utilizing patient -derived material s can 
improve the understanding of cancer biology . Formalin-fixed paraffin -embedded 
(FFPE) samples are the most accessible clinical samples due to their ability to 
preserve tissue structure and cell integrity over long periods. Hematoxylin and eosin 
(H&E) staining, rou tinely employed by pathologists for diagnosis and prognostic 
assessment, offers valuable insights into tissue morphology. Recently, deep learning

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0039
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
rve tissue structure and cell integrity over long periods. Hematoxylin and eosin 
(H&E) staining, rou tinely employed by pathologists for diagnosis and prognostic 
assessment, offers valuable insights into tissue morphology. Recently, deep learning 
models based on H&E whole -slide images (WSIs) have emerged to analyze TME 

## Page 28
 
28 
 features and predict treatment response, but it is still difficult to identify cell types 
with limited information (Yamashita et al., 2021). Besides, traditional methods such 
as immunohistochemistry (IHC) can only achieve low -plex imaging and lack the 
ability to simultaneously explore multiple targets in the TME (Tan et al., 2020). 
On the other hand, recent advancements in multi -omics technologies have facilitated 
large-scale genome, transcriptome, and proteomics analysis of the TME, offering 
novel insights. However, conventional sequencing approaches such as whole genome 
sequencing, bulk RNA sequencing, and single -cell RNA sequencing cannot retain 
spatial information, restricting the understanding of cell interactions and immune 
functions in the TME. To uncover the spatial molecular and functional landscape of 
the TME and resolve gene expression variances at the single -cell level, innovative 
methods and technologies need to be employed to enable spatial exploration across 
the entire ti ssue slide. 
1.4.1 Highly multiplexed imaging 
Highly multiplexed imaging uses antibodies or nucleic acid probes to simultaneously 
detect and visualize multiple molecular targets in sample s to study their spatial 
distribution and complex interactions at high resolution . Mass cytometry imaging 
(MCI) uses antibodies conjugated to heavy metal isotopes to detect specific molecular 
targets.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0040
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
isualize multiple molecular targets in sample s to study their spatial 
distribution and complex interactions at high resolution . Mass cytometry imaging 
(MCI) uses antibodies conjugated to heavy metal isotopes to detect specific molecular 
targets. While it offers exceptionally high subcellular resolution, its application is 
mainly confined by the size of the target area, limiting sele ction to a small region of 
interest (ROIs) in tissue sections (Giesen et al., 2014). Consequently, there's a 
pressing need for multiplexed methods capable of simultaneously labeling multiple 
molecules in a larger area to achieve whole-tissue exploration. 
Multiplex immunohistochemistry (mIHC) utilizes chromogenic or enzymatic 
reactions to visualize multiple protein markers in tissue samples , while m ultiplex 
immunofluorescence (mIF) enables the labeling of protein targets using specifically 
binding antibodies conjugated with fluorophores (Tan et al., 2020). However, limited 
by the spectral overlap of fluorophores and the wavelength range of visible li ght, 

## Page 29
 
29 
 these two methods can only visualize up to 5 different targets in a single cycle. 
Nevertheless, by deactivating, blocking, or stripping labeled antibodies, these two 
techniques can achieve consecutive rounds of iterations, ultimately labeling up to 30 
targets (Lewis et al., 2021). 
In recent years, novel methods have been developed aiming for gentler removal of 
antibody labels to replace the inactivation of the antibodies themselves in traditional 
methods, thereby minimizing tissue damage and expanding the number of available 
cycles. Tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t -CycIF) employs fluorophore 
photobleaching to eliminate the previous fluorophore signal, which greatly reduces

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0041
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
raditional 
methods, thereby minimizing tissue damage and expanding the number of available 
cycles. Tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t -CycIF) employs fluorophore 
photobleaching to eliminate the previous fluorophore signal, which greatly reduces 
the damage to tissue integrity caused by cycles of fluorescent staining and imaging. 
Leveraging a broad array of commercial antibodies against diverse protein targets, t -
CycIF enables very reliable and high -resolution highly multiplexed imaging of FFPE 
slides, accommodating up to 60 different markers (Lin et al., 2018). Another 
approach, co -detection by indexing (CODEX), utilizes primary antibodies conjugated 
to DNA barcodes for staining, with subsequent DNA hybridization facilitating the 
visualization of fluorescent signals (Black et al., 2021). After imaging, reporter 
molecules can be chemically stripped, allowi ng imaging of up to 60 labels. While this 
method allows the scanning of entire tissue sections, its drawbacks include high 
reagent costs and limitations in antibody availability (Lewis et al., 2021). 
1.4.2 Spatial gene expression profiling 
Highly multiplexed imaging enables visualization of the distribution and abundance 
of protein targets within tissue sections, while transcriptome analysis can reveal 
differential gene expression and diverse cellular functional states. As technology 
progresses, spatially resolved gene expression profiling has become attainable. Image -
based in situ multiplex mRNA detection began with the emergence of single -molecule 
fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) in 2008, which employs oligonucleotide 
probes conjug ated with fluorescent tags to identify specific sequences in fixed cells or 
tissues, thereby labeling mRNA molecules (Raj et al., 2008).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0042
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ingle -molecule 
fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) in 2008, which employs oligonucleotide 
probes conjug ated with fluorescent tags to identify specific sequences in fixed cells or 
tissues, thereby labeling mRNA molecules (Raj et al., 2008). In 2014, sequential FISH 

## Page 30
30 (seqFISH) enabled multiple rounds of conti nuous hybridization and imaging, thus 
increasing the number of detectable genes (Lubeck et al., 2014) . To reduce the errors 
accumula ted from repeated mRNA detection, multiplex error-robust FISH 
(MERFISH) was developed in 2015, which uses a combination of tags, including an 
RNA-binding target sequence and a fluorophore-tagged read sequence (Chen et al., 
2015). MERFISH successfully achieves multiplex analysis of the copy number and 
spatial location of up to 1000 RNAs in multiple single cells, but it exhibits limited 
capability in capturing low-abundance transcripts (Walsh & Quail, 2023). Moreover, 
these in situ transcriptomic technologies fall short in capturing and analyzing the 
entire transcriptome profile, posing challenges fo r achieving high-throughput analysis 
at the whole tissue section level. 
In 2019, two mature commercial platf orms emerged for transcriptomic analysis: 
Visium and Ge oMx. Visium Spatial Gene Expression (10x Genomics) is based on in 
situ array that can detect tissue areas of up to 6 mm × 6 mm, with each spot offering a 
resolution of approximately 100 μm (2-10 cells) (Ståhl et al., 2016). The V isium 
workflow begins with H&E staining and imaging of fre sh frozen sections . Followed 
by tissue permeabilizatio n, beads with spatially barcoded oligonuc leotides are used to 
achieve efficient capture of mRNA and preserve spatial information. Collected mRNA

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0043
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
begins with H&E staining and imaging of fre sh frozen sections . Followed 
by tissue permeabilizatio n, beads with spatially barcoded oligonuc leotides are used to 
achieve efficient capture of mRNA and preserve spatial information. Collected mRNA 
undergoes reverse transcription and NGS to generate spatial transcriptomics data, 
providing a high-resolution view of the transcriptome (Ståhl et al., 2016) . Visium has 
good sensitivity and can capture a wide range of genes with low-level transcript 
expression, making it suitable for FFPE sections as well (Nerurkar et al., 2020). Since 
each measurement site contains multiple cells, whole transcriptome reads of Visium 
require deconvolut ion using algorithms to determine cell type (Seferbekova et al., 
2023). Widely utilized in spatial transcriptomic analyses of various cancers such as 
breast cancer, prosta te cancer, and malignant mela noma, Visium has unveiled 
unprecedented intra- a nd inter-tumor heterogeneit y, along with distinctive gene 
expression profile s (Nerurkar e t al., 2020). A recent study leverage d Visium spatial 
transcriptomics to successfully identify multiple tumor subclones with distinct copy 

## Page 31
 
31 
 number variations in a single HGSC section, showcasing varied infiltration patterns 
and cellular interactions (Denisenko et al., 2024). 
On the other hand , GeoMx Digital Spatial Profiler (NanoString) utilizes probes with 
photocleavable barcodes to bind to corresponding mRNA in FFPE tissue . It then 
illuminates the selected ROIs with UV light to release all oligonucleotide probes in 
this region for quantitative NGC sequencing analysis (Merritt et al., 2020). Tumor and 
immune cell specific antibodies are used in this process to morphologically label

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0044
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ates the selected ROIs with UV light to release all oligonucleotide probes in 
this region for quantitative NGC sequencing analysis (Merritt et al., 2020). Tumor and 
immune cell specific antibodies are used in this process to morphologically label 
tissues to assist in ROIs selection. NanoString provides probe sets for both the 1,833 -
gene canc er transcriptome atlas (CTA) genome and the 18,000 -gene human 
transcriptome, thus enabling large -scale spatial transcriptomic analysis (Moses & 
Pachter, 2022). GeoMx has been used to study TME heterogeneity, biomarkers of ICI 
treatment, and TLS mechanisms in melanoma (van Dam et al., 2022; Toki et al., 
2019). GeoMx is highly sensitive and customized individual areas of illumination 
(AOIs) in a single ROIs can be generated using morphological markers, which 
enables gene expression analysis for specific cell types or spatial locations. However, 
it's important to note the subjectivity inherent in ROIs and AOI selection. In addition, 
while targeted methods enhance detection sensitivity, they are limited by the number 
of genes in the probes combination set and their high cost (Wang et al., 2021). 
Released in 2022, the spatial molecular imaging system CosMx (NanoString) aims to 
achieve high -resolution spatial analysis of RNA and proteins in tissues at the single -
cell or subcellular level. CosMx utilizes an innovative approach by combining an in 
situ hybridization (ISH) probe with a fluorescent readout probe, known as a reporter, 
to capture RNA within sample tissue. The distinct fluorescent sequence of the reporter 
enables precise spot detection at high resolution. This system is capable of performing 
multiple cycles of fluorescent molecular barcode hybridization with high sensitivity

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0045
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
A within sample tissue. The distinct fluorescent sequence of the reporter 
enables precise spot detection at high resolution. This system is capable of performing 
multiple cycles of fluorescent molecular barcode hybridization with high sensitivity 
and low error rates at the single -cell level (He et al., 2022). CosMx has a large 
scanning area (16 –375 mm2) for tissue sections and eliminates the need for additional 
tissue permeabilization. Serving as an automated integrated platform, CosMx enables 

## Page 32
 
32 
 three-dimensional super -resolution localization of FFPE sections containing up to 2 
million cells, coupled with cell segmentation based on morphological antibodies (He 
et al., 2022). Preliminary studies have demonstrated CosMx’s efficacy in showing the 
co-localization of B cells and Tfh cells in GCs in lymph nodes, as well as IL18 
signaling between B cells and macrophages (Hether et al., 2022). These findings 
underscore CosMx's ability to unravel immune processes in small tissue structures . 
Pick-seq (Rarecyte) is a new method for in -depth spatial transcription analysis of 
tissue sections, particularly FFPE samples. It mainly involves micro -region extraction 
and subsequent gene expression profiling or sequencing. Using microneedles with a 
diameter of 25 or 40 µm, microtissue regions containing 5 -20 cells can be isolated 
from tissue sections and collected, thereby preserving spatial information for 
subsequent transcriptome or proteomics analysis (Podyminogin et al., 2019 ). 
Microtissue extractio n relies on low -plex immunofluorescence staining with 
commercial antibodies on adjacent tissue sections, followed by whole -slide imaging 
by CyteFinder®. Through the usage of this instrument, it is possible to capture cells

vector_chunks.text:
Microtissue extractio n relies on low -plex immunofluorescence staining with 
commercial antibodies on adjacent tissue sections, followed by whole -slide imaging 
by CyteFinder®. Through the usage of this instrument, it is possible to capture cells 
from any area of a whole tissue section for whole transcriptome isolation, 
amplification, and sequencing. Previous studies have applied pick -seq for spatial 
transcriptomic analysis of melanoma and breast cancer (Maliga et al., 2021; Ericson et 
al., 2020), showing its efficacy in exploring spatial gene expression. However, to date, 
there have been no studies that conduct pick-seq and perform rapid gene expression 
analysis through non -sequencing methods. This presents an area ripe for exploration 
to further assist with spatial gene expression profiling.

## Page 33
 
33 
 2 Introduction 
The clinical treatment of ovarian cancer faces significant challenges due to its high 
mortality rate and limited treatment options. Most patients are diagnosed with ovarian 
cancer at advanced stages, resulting in poor overall survival. Current standard 
treatments including p rimary debulking surgery and NACT fall short in efficacy , 
lacking consistent positive outcomes across all cases. In this context, new treatments 
using targeted therapies and ICIs have shown promising clinical results but have 
limited improvement in overall survival. Considering the suppressive immune 
network and functionally restricted TILs in the TME of ovarian cancer, it is necessary 
to understand more the role of adaptive immunity in anti -tumor immune response s 
with spatial characteristics . This will reveal their impact on clinical therapeutic effects 
and help improve current ICI treatments . 
TLSs are widely present in the TME of ovarian cancer but exhibit notable inter -
individual heterogeneity. While prior research has explored the predictive power of 
TLSs in tumor progression and patient prognosis, many anti -tumor immune 
mechanisms underlying these processes remain unclear. This knowledge gap indicates 
that TLS is a promising research field that should be further explored in mechanisms 
of its formation, maintenance, and interaction with TME. Moreover, it is still under 
debate whether the presence and abundance of TLSs favor patients’ positive immune 
responses and prognoses (Kang et al., 2021). Given such a double -edged sword role 
of TLS in anti -tumor immunit y, analyzing spatially resolved gene expression in

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0048
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
debate whether the presence and abundance of TLSs favor patients’ positive immune 
responses and prognoses (Kang et al., 2021). Given such a double -edged sword role 
of TLS in anti -tumor immunit y, analyzing spatially resolved gene expression in 
mature TLS areas of clinical samples holds significant potential and value. 
New highly multiplexed imaging methods, such as t-CycIF, have significantly 
advanced the spatial analysis of the TME in ovarian cancer across entire tissue slides. 
Combining t -CycIF and high -resolution imaging, spatial image analysis at the single -
cell level has been achieved. This innovative approach has led to the identification of 
positive prognostic markers for the combination therapy of PARP inhibitors and PD -1 
inhibitors in platinum -resistant patients (Färkkilä et al., 2020), as well as the 

## Page 34
 
34 
 discovery of selective spatial attraction and interactions among immune cell 
subpopulations in TLS s in the TME of ovarian cancer (Sarkkinen et al., 2023). These 
findings underscore the crucial role of integrating the spatial dimension in cancer 
immunology research. 
While t-CycIF offers valuable insights into cell phenotype and neighborhood 
interactions with the help of validated antibodies, it lacks the ability to provide deeper 
molecular -level gene expression analysis. Spatial transcriptomics techniques such as 
GeoMx and Visium address this limitation by capturing mRNA and enabling 
sequencing analysis, allowing for the integration of cellular and molecular features 
with spatial resolution. Furthermore, pick -seq offers the possibility for microtissue 
collection of distinct cell populations and consequent high-throughput spatial analysis,

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0049
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
lysis, allowing for the integration of cellular and molecular features 
with spatial resolution. Furthermore, pick -seq offers the possibility for microtissue 
collection of distinct cell populations and consequent high-throughput spatial analysis, 
which is particularly crucial for studying microstructures like TLS s in the TME . 
However, despite its tremendous research potential , there are still many issues 
regarding picking efficiency , the need for high tissue quality, and high costs for 
sequencing analysis . Therefore, it is necessary to develop a novel method using pick -
seq to achieve spatially resolved gene profiling with high resolution and low costs . 
This method will comprehensively enhance the investigation of anti-tumor immunity 
mechanisms in the TME of ovarian cancer. 
2.1 Significance and challenges 
The optimized picking in this study can achieve precise and efficient extraction of 40 
μm diameter microtissues targeting different cell types with accurate spatial locations. 
When it is applied to the TLS s of ovarian cancer on FFPE slides and combined with 
the Nanostring nCounter Panel for gene expression profiling, this novel method has 
great potential to reveal the mechanism s of anti-tumor immune responses and 
potential key regulation genes cost -effectively . Given that FFPE slides can be stored 
for long periods and are easily available, it is possible now to implement spatially 
resolved large -scale transcriptome or genome analysis with large clinical sample 
cohorts across time. 

## Page 35
 
35 
 Currently, there are several key challenges for this new method. While techniques like 
pick-seq and Visium have been successfully utilized for fresh frozen samples,

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
nome analysis with large clinical sample 
cohorts across time. 

## Page 35
 
35 
 Currently, there are several key challenges for this new method. While techniques like 
pick-seq and Visium have been successfully utilized for fresh frozen samples, 
adapting a new technique for application on FFPE samples remains unexplored. 
Given the typically inferior RNA quality in FFPE slides, a range of obstacles must be 
addressed to guarantee both the yield and integrity of extracted mRNA. Due to the 
extremely low amount of mRNA yield obtained through pick-seq, the current 
approach relies solely on co stly and time -consuming sequencing methods after pre-
amplification . There is no method to use gene panels for fast targeted immune 
analysis. Besides, a lthough the nCounter PanCancer gene panel can detect gene 
expression levels for up to 770 genes, the lack of spatial information obscures key 
differentially expressed genes. Therefore, n ew protocols need to be designed to ensure 
that spatial information is preserved during microtissue extraction for subsequent 
transcriptomic data analysis. 
2.2 Research aims 
The primary research aim of this master's thesis is to establish a novel method called 
Nano-Pick that enables efficient and consistent operation of optimized pick-seq and 
verifies its suitability for Nanostring nCounter gene panel analysis. The following 
goals will be pursued and achieved: 
1: Improve ROIs selection in pick -seq experiments through TLSs annotation from t -
CycIF images 
1.1: Utilize t -CycIF stainings to identify and annotate TLSs regions in the TME of 
ovarian cancer 
1.2: Optimize antibody staining protocols to achieve successful cell type 
recognition for pick-seq

3.1.2 Patients and specimens 
All FFPE tissue samples are obtained from the ONCOSYS -Ova prospective study or 
the Helsinki University Hospital (HUH) tissue block collection. These blocks are 
taken from interval debulking surgery after NACT treatment, or from primary 
debulking surgery of HGSC patients. All samples utilized in this study, along with 
their corresponding clinical data, have received approval for research purposes from 
the ethics committees of the Women's Clinic, Helsinki University Hospital (HUH), 
and Helsinki Biobank. Patie nt IDs and other personal information are handled with 
utmost security and confidentiality on the HUH server, utilizing the HUH Datalake

and Helsinki Biobank. Patie nt IDs and other personal information are handled with

infrastructure provided by the Helsinki Biobank. Access to and extraction of clinical 
data are strictly limited to auth orized personnel only. In the publicly published results, 
each patient is assigned a randomized publication ID to minimize the possibility of 
patient identification. 
In this study, 17 samples with multiple TLSs are selected from 17 patients. Details of 
these samples are shown in Table 3.

3.2 Methods 
3.2.1 Sample sectioning 
Tissues are processed and embedded into paraffin blocks at the Tissue Preparation and 
Histochemistry Unit (TPHU) within the Anatomy Department at Biomedicum. Tissue 
sections with 5 μm thickness are cut from FFPE blocks at the histopathology services 
of the Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland (FIMM). For each sample, 7 adjacent 
tissue sections are obtained and then the best three adjacent slides are chosen for t -
CyCIF and microtissue picking ( Figure 1).

## Page 40
 
40 
 3.2.2 t-CycIF protocol 
3.2.2.1 Deparaffinization, rehydration, antigen retrieval and pre -staining 
First, paraffin around the tissue needs to be completely removed from all sectioned 
samples. The drying oven is preheated to 56℃. All the selected slides are placed 
vertically on the plastic slide holder at a certain distance and then baked in the drying 
oven for 30 minutes. After that, all FFPE slides were submerged in xylene s olution 
three times for 5 minutes each to remove paraffin. 
Next, all slides were put in a series of ethanol solutions with gradually decreasing 
concentrations (100% ethanol, 90% ethanol, 70% ethanol, 50% ethanol, 30% 
ethanol), once for each concentration lasting 3 minutes. This process can gradually 
rehydrate samp les. Finally, wash twice in 1xPBS for one minute each to remove any 
residual ethanol. 
In order to fully retrieve the cross -linked antigen molecules after being treated with 
FFPE, all slides need to be placed in a glass container filled with 250 ml of freshly 
made 10M citric acid solution (pH=6) and heated in the microwave to 100℃. After 
the heating process, the slides are allowed to cool down slowly at room temperature 
for 20 minutes. Then, Odyssey blocking buffer was added to the tissue. All slides 
were incubated at room temperature for 30 minutes in a moist incubation chamber to 
prevent ev aporation and tissue dehydration.

ool down slowly at room temperature 
for 20 minutes. Then, Odyssey blocking buffer was added to the tissue. All slides 
were incubated at room temperature for 30 minutes in a moist incubation chamber to 
prevent ev aporation and tissue dehydration. 
Next, use 1:5000 Hoechst and 1:1000 goat -anti-rabbit secondary antibodies for pre -
staining for 1 hour at room temperature. When finished, wash three times with 1xPBS 
for three minutes each time to remove residual antibodies. To remove background 
signals, the antibodies bound to the tissue during pre -stain were bleached to inactivate 
their fluorophores (see 3.2.2.3) for 1.5 hours. Finally, the slides are washed four times 
in 1xPBS for 3 minutes each to completely remove residual solution from the 
bleaching p rocess. 
 

## Page 41
 
41 
 3.2.2.2 Antibody staining 
To identify the TLS s regions and analyze the different types of immune cells in the 
TME, an antibody panel was designed and applied in 5 cycles of t -CycIF experiments 
(Table 4). In the first cycle, an unconjugated BCL6 antibody (rabbit) was used with 
overnight incubation at 4°C. Then on the following day, samples are incubated by 
goat-anti-rabbit secondary antibodies conjugated with AlexaFluor -488 (1:2000) and 
Hoechst (1:10000) in Odyssey buffer for 1 hour at room temperature. After staining, 
the slides were washed three times with 1xPBS, three minutes each. Then 70µl of 
70% glycerol was added and each slide was covered with a coverslip, ready for 
scanning and imaging using Rare cyte CyteFinder® II with a magnification of 20X . 
The second to fifth cycles all used primary antibodies conjugated with either Alexa 
Fluor or eFluor. In each cycle, the slides were incubated overnight at 4°C with the

nning and imaging using Rare cyte CyteFinder® II with a magnification of 20X . 
The second to fifth cycles all used primary antibodies conjugated with either Alexa 
Fluor or eFluor. In each cycle, the slides were incubated overnight at 4°C with the 
corresponding antibodies, and then scan ned in the next day after washing and 
mounting. 
3.2.2.3 Fluorophore inactivation (bleaching) 
Fluorophore inactivation is required between each staining cycle to ensure the 
removal of all fluorescent markers from the previous cycle. The solution used for 
bleaching needs to be prepared freshly containing 40ml 1x PBS, 7.5ml 30% H2O2 
and 2.5ml 480 mM NaOH. This 50ml bleach solution can be used for up to 7 slides. 
The slides are placed into a plastic container with the bleach solution. Two large -area 
LED light panels are then used to photobleach the tissues double -sided at room 
temperature. The total bl eaching time of each cycle is 90 minutes, of which the 
bleaching solution needs to be replaced every 30 minutes following pre -stain, and 45 
minutes for other cycles.

3.2.3 Images acquisition, processing , and visualization 
3.2.3.1 Images acquisition 
Images were acquired using a Rarecyte CyteFinder ® II high-throughput scanner. This 
is an automated microscope platform for digital imaging of slides, inside containing 
the slide holder and multiple microscope lenses with different magnifications (4X, 
10X, 20X, 40X). This imaging system also consists of a series of software connected 
to the scanner, including RareCyte Instrument Controller (operates the microscope), 
CyteHub (control center for scanning and image analysis), CyteFinder (set up and

to the scanner, including RareCyte Instrument Controller (operates the microscope), 
CyteHub (control center for scanning and image analysis), CyteFinder (set up and 
monitor the scan progress), and Imager (for real real -time imaging and picking 
control). Up to four antibodies can be scanned simultaneously in one cycle and they 
should be located in different channels (488nm, 555nm, 647nm, 750nm). Hoechst 
33342 is consistently used in each cycle for nuclear staining. CyteFinder® II can scan 
two slides together within 30 minutes with a magnification of 20X, offering excellent 
scanning efficiency and well -focused high -quality images.

 The imaging process begins by placing the prepared slide upside down on the slide 
holder because all microscope lenses are inverted under the slide holder. Before 
scanning, an essay suitable for this experiment needs to be set up, including the 
antibodies used in each channel, the appropriate exposure time, and a series of other 
scanning-related parameters. When scanning a slide for the first time, a pre -scan 
needs to be conducted to identify the tissue area, allowing for manually selecting the 
appropriate scanning range and adding focusing points at different locations in 
CyteHub. The selected scan range and focusing points are stored in the CyteHub 
system. Starting from the second cycle, the pre -defined same area saved for each slide 
can be automatically s elected and scanned, which ensures the overlay of several 
images from different cycles. 
After successfully focusing on the tissue slide, scanning is performed automatically 
according to a pre -designed whole -area grid containing hundreds of micro -regions, 
called tiles. During scanning, all channels of a tile are imaged in sequence before 
moving to the next tile. These tiles have partially overlapped areas with each other, 
which facilitates the stitching process later. Upon completion of each scanning cycle,

es. During scanning, all channels of a tile are imaged in sequence before 
moving to the next tile. These tiles have partially overlapped areas with each other, 
which facilitates the stitching process later. Upon completion of each scanning cycle, 
the raw data containing all tiles is stored in the corresponding rcpnl file (Figure 2). 
3.2.3.2 Images processing and visualization 
To assemble all tiles into a high-dimensional image, an open-source software package 
ASHLAR (Alignment by Simultaneous Harmonization of Layer / Adjacency 
Registration) is used for the stitching and alignment process of all images from 
different cycles (Figure 2). ASHLAR 's workflow mainly consists of three steps: 
stitching together adjacent tiles in each imaging cycle; checking the relative positions 
of the same tiles across different cycles compared to the first cycle , and then 
registration ; output a final ome.tiff image containing all channels and all cycles . This 
process of stitching and registration is based only on the nuclear staining of the 
Hoechst channel because the nuclei have clear position s and boundaries to be 
identified easily. Similar to a pyramid structure, t he ome.tiff image uses an image 

## Page 44
44 stacking method that contains multiple layers of images with different resolutions. 
This optimizes the speed of visualization and image processing of such high-
dimensional images, as only a partial image within the field of view of a single 
resolution is loaded at each moment. 
Figure 2 : The schematic diagram of image processing and visualization . 
Napari, a fast and interactive viewer for multi -dimensional images in Python, is used 
to visualize the stitched images ( Figure 2). It can help view ome.tiff images and allow

gure 2 : The schematic diagram of image processing and visualization . 
Napari, a fast and interactive viewer for multi -dimensional images in Python, is used 
to visualize the stitched images ( Figure 2). It can help view ome.tiff images and allow 
for the overlay of specific local area annotations or cell segmentations. The antibody 
information of all channels is pre -stored in a txt file and loaded into Napari alongside 
the image to display the antibody name corresponding to each channel. Adjusted 
contrast limits for each channel can be saved and recalled when the image is reloaded. 
3.2.4 TLS s identification and ROIs selection 
All TLSs are identified based on the high expression of CD20, CD4, and PD1 in t -
CycIF images. The contrast limits for different markers were adjusted and saved in 
Napari for optimized visualization. Round or oval aggregation centers with very 
strong CD20 e xpression were identified as GCs. Mature TLSs also have abundantly 
surrounded CD4+ T cells with high expression of PD1 on partial cells. Aggregated

45 areas of CD20+ B cells that lack CD4 and PD1 expression and have no obvious T/B 
cell compartmental structure are immature TLSs. 
Rectangle shapes are used in Napari to mark regions of all mature TLSs including 
adjacent tumor cells. All added location areas are stored separately for each sample. A 
custom Python script is applied to automatically crop and export all TLSs images for 
further TLSs annotation and ROIs selection. 
Next, annotations of three different zones are marked in each TLS region using 
QuPath. Since the picking targets are quite small, these annotations do not have to be

crop and export all TLSs images for 
further TLSs annotation and ROIs selection. 
Next, annotations of three different zones are marked in each TLS region using 
QuPath. Since the picking targets are quite small, these annotations do not have to be 
fixed shapes and the only goal is to optimally label different cell types and 
interactions . First, check all TLSs images and only choose those mature TLSs with 
clear GC. Then the red line was drawn to mark the outer ring area of GCs without the 
center of only CD20+B cells. A large number of PD1+CD4+T cells and B cells are 
located in these T –B cell zones, serving as the primary site where lymphocytes 
coordinate immune functions within TLSs. Myeloid – T cell zones around GCs were 
then annotated with the green line, which contains abundant CD4+T cells, CD8+T 
cells, macrophages, DCs, and B cells. Th ese zones are identified as key areas where 
immune responses such as antigen presentation occur between myeloid cells and T 
cells with rich cell -to-cell interactions. Surrounded tumor zones were finally 
annotated with yellow lines, which also contain small numbers of cytotoxic T cells, 
macrophages, and DCs. 
Based on all annotations of TLSs, five ROIs were selected in each different zone in a 
single TLS area. The aim is to select ROIs with cell -cell interactions so that further 
gene expression analysis can be done to make discoveries. The diameter of all ROIs is 
40µm, which is consistent with the diameter of the microneedle used in the actual 
operation. The minimum distance between adjacent ROIs is 40µm to reserve enough 
space for the wiggling process during the picking. All selected ROIs and their 
coordinates were stored in the Rarecyte CyteHub® system, waiting to be transferred

operation. The minimum distance between adjacent ROIs is 40µm to reserve enough

 3.2.5 Microtissue extraction 
3.2.5.1 Antibody staining without antigen retrieval 
FFPE slides adjacent to t -CycIF slides were selected for microtissue extraction. These 
slides also need to undergo a similar slide preparation process as the t -CycIF 
experiment, including deparaffinization in xylene and rehydration in ethanol series 
with gradually decreasing concentrations. To bett er protect the mRNA in the FFPE 
sample, all ethanol series are prepared with DEPC-treated water on ice with 0.0025% 
RNasin Plus. Antigen retrieval will not be performed next. In the process of antigen 
retrieval in FFPE samples, conventional methods need to use heat-induced antigen 
retrieval methods (HIER) to remove protein cross -links and expose antigens, which 
can significantly improve the efficiency of antibody binding and staining results 
(Krenacs et al., 2010). However, extracting mRNA from FFPE samples requires 
avoiding high -temperature processing to preserve the integrity of the mRNA as much 
as possible. Therefore, the antigen retrieval step was skipped, while the staining steps 
were optimized with reliable antibodies to compensate for the loss of stai ning results 
as much as possible. 
The blocking was reduced to 10 minutes at room temperature with Odyssey buffer 
containing 0.1% RNasin Plus. All samples were then directly stained by Hoechst and 
pre-conjugated antibodies including Iba1, PanCK, and CD45 in the real picking 
experiment .

locking was reduced to 10 minutes at room temperature with Odyssey buffer 
containing 0.1% RNasin Plus. All samples were then directly stained by Hoechst and 
pre-conjugated antibodies including Iba1, PanCK, and CD45 in the real picking 
experiment . These antibodies are validated from a test experiment with a designed 
antibody panel (Table 5). 0.1% RNasin Plus was always added to the antibody mix. 
The slides are incubated with antibodies at room temperature for 10 minutes and then 
moved to the 4°C fridge overnight.

47 3.2.5.2 Scan images and ROI s transfer 
On the next day, the slides were washed in 1xPBS with 0.0025% RNasin Plus for five 
minutes. All PBS used in this experiment is made with sterile 10xPBS stock and 
DEPC-treated water. Then 50µl of 50% glycerol was added and each slide was 
covered with a coverslip . Scan the slides using Rarecyte CyteFinder® II with a 
magnification of 10X .

vector_chunks.text:
All PBS used in this experiment is made with sterile 10xPBS stock and 
DEPC-treated water. Then 50µl of 50% glycerol was added and each slide was 
covered with a coverslip . Scan the slides using Rarecyte CyteFinder® II with a 
magnification of 10X . All images automatically undergo illumination correction, 
tissue distortion correction , and stitching in CyteHub system to generate stitched 
ome.tiff images. These ome.tiff images can be viewed in the Tissue Results in 
CyteHub. 
All pre-selected ROIs were then transferred from the adjacent t -CycIF image to this 
picking slide . There were only very minor tissue differences between adjacent 
sections. All ROIs were reviewed and corrected manually to ensure their correct 
positions on target cells . After that, ROIs were stored in the CyteHub and they can be 
further transferred automatically from this up -side-down scanning image to the real 
picking position on the upward tissue. 
After scanning, the slides were merged in a box of sterile 1x PBS with 0.0025% 
RNasin to remove the coverslip and then washed in sterile 1x PBS with 0.0025% 
RNasin for 3 min to remove residual glycerol. 
3.2.5.3 Picking preparation 
There are 6 small nuclease-free PCR tubes on the needle holder. These PCR tubes are 
specially designed by Rarecyte with a cross on the flat bottom to facilitate focusing 
and visualization of deposited cells. Before start ing picking, d ifferent solutions are 
added to these 6 tubes as shown in Table 6:

Next, load all six needles into the needle holder. Set the needle holder on the right of 
the slide holder within the machine, while loading an empty slide on the left with 
fingerprints for calibration. Select and load a needle, then flush it three times in the 
rinsing solution before moving it to the calibration slide. The calibration is very 
important to ensure precise control of the needle. Both automatic calibration and 
manual calibration can be chosen. A successful calibration can align the actual 
position (x, y) of the needle tip with the displayed position (x, y) shown on the Imager 
interface. Sample positions can also be calibrated to ensure the needle tip precisely 
touches the slide (z position) as indicated in the needle controller. A successful 
calibration will return a combination of parameters for slide z, needle z, and CPZ. 
They should be around -1000, -1170, and 26900 respectively. 
Finally, import saved ROIs within the Imager Main Control . The files with the 
extensions .rcpickmap and .metadata need to be load ed. Now all ROIs have been 
transferred to the right positions and picking prepar ation is ready.

## Page 49
 
49 
 3.2.5.4 Picking & collection 
Check all picking parameters and select Hoechst as the focusing channel. There are 
several steps in a single picking process. The needle needs to be flushed in the rinsing 
solution three times and then filled with retrieval buffer. Next, move the needle to the 
target ROIS and complete picking. The extracted microtissue should be deposited 
immediately after picking . Repeat this process up to five times to complete the 
picking for one zone of TLS annotations. Before continuing to pick for another zone, 
the slide should be put back to 1xPBS with 0.1% RNasin Plus for three minutes to 
avoid tissue dehydration. 
The aim is to collect 10 picks of the same zone into one deposit tube. Therefore, a 
total of 30 picks should be done for one slide. If the interval exceeds half an hour or 
there is a significant deviation in the picking position, the needle needs to be re -
calibrated. After ten picks of each zone are completed, the tube is immediately stored 
in a -80°C refrigerator for subsequent tissue lysis and mRNA extraction. 
3.2.6 mRNA extraction 
3.2.6.1 Tissue digestion 
PCR tubes containing extracted microtissues were removed from the freezer and 
thawed at room temperature for five minutes. All tubes were then incubated at 56°C in 
a PCR thermocycler for one hour to lyse the tissue and release mRNA. Upon 
completion , the tubes were briefly vortexed, spun down, and placed on ice. 
3.2.6.2 mRNA extraction and purification 
The first step is beads preparation. The original NorthernMax buffer (10×) was taken

r to lyse the tissue and release mRNA. Upon 
completion , the tubes were briefly vortexed, spun down, and placed on ice. 
3.2.6.2 mRNA extraction and purification 
The first step is beads preparation. The original NorthernMax buffer (10×) was taken 
out from the fridge to be well-mixed. New 2× NorthernMax buffer and 1× 
NorthernMax buffer were made in a 10 ml falcon tube. The Dynabeads Oligo (dT) 25 
beads were resuspended in the vial . 120 μl (for 6 samples) of beads were transferred 
to a 1.5ml Eppendorf tube and washed three times with 1 ml ice -cold 1×hybridization 
buffer (1×NorthernMax buffer including 0.05% Tween 20 and 0.0025% RNasin Plus ). 

## Page 50
50 Wash for 1 minute, put the tube on a magnet for 1 minute, and remove all buffers each 
time. After washing, resuspend the beads in the original bead volume with 120 μl ice-
cold 2× hybridization buffer (2× NorthernMax buffer including 0. 1% Tween 20 and 
0.01% RNasin Plus). 
To capture mRNA, 20 μl of washed beads were added to each lysed sample . They 
were incubated at 56°C for 1 minute and at room temperature for 10 minutes. Samples 
were placed on a magnet and a ll supernatants were removed . Now all mRNA should 
be combined with the beads. 
The beads were washed twice with 150 μl ice-cold 1× hybridization buffer. Wash for 
1 minute, put on magnet for 1 minute , and remove all supernatants. Then wash the 
beads once with 150 μl ice-cold 1× PBS with 0.0025% RNasin Plus. Wash for 1 
minute, put on magnet for 1 minute, and remove all supernatants. To elute mRNA, all 
the pellets were resuspended in 10.5 μl nuclease-free water in 0.2ml PCR tubes. The 
tubes were incubated at 80°C for 2 minutes and immediately placed on a magnet. All

te, put on magnet for 1 minute, and remove all supernatants. To elute mRNA, all 
the pellets were resuspended in 10.5 μl nuclease-free water in 0.2ml PCR tubes. The 
tubes were incubated at 80°C for 2 minutes and immediately placed on a magnet. All 
supernatants were transferred to new RNase -Free PCR tubes on ice, waiting for 
reverse transcription. 
3.2.6.3 Reverse transcription 
SuperScript IV VILO Master Mix was used for fast reverse transcription. 8 μl mRNA 
template and 2 μl 5X master mix were added to an empty RNase -free 0.2ml PCR tube. 
All tubes were placed in a PCR thermocycler with this program: 10 minutes to anneal 
primers at 25℃, 10 minutes to reverse transcribe mRNA at 50℃, and 5 minutes to 
inactivate enzyme at 85℃. Remove all tubes from the PCR thermal cycler and place 
them on ice. cDNA products can be stored at -20℃ for up to a week and -80℃ for 
long-term storage. 
3.2.6.4 qPCR 
qPCR experiments were run on an Applied Biosystems 7500 Fast Instrument. Three 
primers, GAPDH, RPS13, and B2M were used, which are considered to be highly 

## Page 51
51 expressed genes in all cells ( Table 7). The reagent composition for each well in the 
qPCR experiment is detailed in Table 8. A total of 5 samples were analyzed, with 2 -3 
replicates for each primer per sample. Sample information on the 96 -well plate is 
provided in Table 9. Negative control contained all reagents except for the cDNA 
template, which was replaced with an equal volume of water.

4.1 Traditional t -CyCIF staining s successfully guide TLS identification 
According to the standard experimental protocols of t -CyCIF, all samples were 
stained for 4 cycles using the same antibody set and concentration ( Table 4). Two 
mature tumor -surrounded TLS regions from the same sample were selected to show 
the staining results ( Figure 3). Whole-section tissue images of all samples were 
accurately stitched together using ASHLAR, with no overlapping or missing areas. 
No obvious tissue shift, tissue deformation, or detachment were found in images from 
different cycles.

4.2 Iba1, PanCK, and CD45 stainings without antigen retrieval enable the 
recognition of distinct cell populations 
The slides adjacent to the t -CycIF slides are used for actual microtissue extraction. 
The traditional pretreatment process utilizes high temperatures for antigen retrieval , 
which can better expose all antigens and enhance the staining effect. In order to avoid 
damage to mRNA quality caused by high temperature , staining protocols were 
optimized to achieve close staining effects without antigen retrieval (Shown in 
methods).

n better expose all antigens and enhance the staining effect. In order to avoid 
damage to mRNA quality caused by high temperature , staining protocols were 
optimized to achieve close staining effects without antigen retrieval (Shown in 
methods). 
As shown in Figure 5, the staining quality of Iba1 and CD45 did not exhibit 
significant degradation in the absence of antigen retrieval. However, there were 
instances of antibody accumulation and incomplete cell staining in edge areas with 
suboptimal resolution. PanCK effectively labeled most cancer cells, yet it displayed 
noticeable variations in signal intensity across different regions with uneven 
fluorescence signals. Additionally, CK7 was also used to label cancer cells, but 
compared w ith PanCK, the staining results were notably inferior, with very low and 
severely uneven fluorescence intensity. Overall, despite the decreased signal intensity 
observed, Iba1, PanCK, and CD45 still showed satisfactory staining results, 
adequately facilitating the identification of diverse cell types and tissue structures in 
TLSs. The suboptimal staining quality in certain areas minimally impacts the 
execution of picking procedures.

 4.3 Optimized parameter setting schemes achieve consistent microtissue 
extraction for different cell phenotypes 
Due to the pioneering nature of pick -seq technology, there has been a lack of 
published methods providing specific parameter combinations for efficient

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0073
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
eter setting schemes achieve consistent microtissue 
extraction for different cell phenotypes 
Due to the pioneering nature of pick -seq technology, there has been a lack of 
published methods providing specific parameter combinations for efficient 
microtissue extraction from FFPE slides. Rarecyte's user manual offers only a broad 
range of available p arameter settings for each parameter. Based on this, the optimized 
parameter combination for microtissue extraction in FFPE slides was determined and 
shown here for the first time after numerous experiments ( Table 10). A series of 
optimized distance parameters on the left ensure precise microneedle positioning for 
effective extraction and deposition of the target microtissue. On the right, the 
optimized volume parameters are crucial for achieving a high extraction success rate 
and optimal collection efficiency. In addition, different strategies for wiggling are 
needed for different tissue types to promote the detachment of the target microtissue 
from the surroun ding tissue. 
Table 10: Optimized Picking Parameters 
Pick Standby 150 µm Fill Needle (FN) 210 nL 
Wet Standby 0 µm Wet Volume (WV) 160 nL 
Overdrive 10 µm Fill Buffer (FB) 0 nL 
Backoff 0 µm Draw Volume (DV) 100 nL 
Deposit Distance 500 µm Dispense (DP) 150 nL 
 Dispense Rate 100% 
Wiggle Radius 30 µm Stage Wiggles 2 or 3 
Specifically, 150 µm Pick Standby guarantees a safe distance for FFPE slices before 
picking. The 0 µm Wet Standby prevents wet droplets from displacing cells and 
compromising tissue integrity. With a 10 µm Overdrive, the needle tip maintains close 
contact with the FFPE section without damaging the needle, promoting effective 
tissue separation during the wiggle process. Employing a 0 µm Backoff ensures

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0074
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
nd 
compromising tissue integrity. With a 10 µm Overdrive, the needle tip maintains close 
contact with the FFPE section without damaging the needle, promoting effective 
tissue separation during the wiggle process. Employing a 0 µm Backoff ensures 
selective aspiration of only the target micro -tissues, avoiding extraction of tissues 

## Page 58
 
58 
 outside the ROIs. Deposit Distance of 500 µm enables efficient collection of the 
extracted microtissues into the deposit tube. 
Throughout the entire picking process, the liquid volume within the microneedle tube 
undergoes multiple changes. Therefore, different volume parameters need to be well 
combined to ensure optimal wetting, strong suction, and a 100% dispense rate to 
collect all extracted cells. Following practical exploration, the calculation formula of 
the relationship between volume parameters is summarized: 
FN = WV + FB + DP - DV 
Considering the slight variation in the total volume of microneedle tubes across 
different needles, a universal volume of FN=210 nL serves as a more appropriate 
choice, nearing the upper limit to maximize tube utilization. With a 40 µm diameter 
microneedle , the Draw Volume was first determined to be 100 nL. This is because 
when the microtissue is suitable for extraction, a higher DV increases the probability 
of aspirating surrounding tissue, while a lower DV reduces the success rate of target 
tissue extract ion in each ROIs. Subsequently, it was found in control experiments that 
the Wet Volume of 160 nL best matched the DV of 100 nL, which can maximize the 
extraction success rate and accuracy. Finally, the Dispense was set at 150 nL, ensuring 
100% discharge of the extracted t issue into the dispense tube. No additional Fill 
Buffer is required.

vector_chunks.text:
best matched the DV of 100 nL, which can maximize the 
extraction success rate and accuracy. Finally, the Dispense was set at 150 nL, ensuring 
100% discharge of the extracted t issue into the dispense tube. No additional Fill 
Buffer is required. 
Figure 6 illustrates the schematic diagram of the picking procedure using the 
optimized parameter combination. For cancer cells with stronger cell adhesion, Stage 
Wiggles can be set to 3 to improve the extraction success rate. Every time a 
microtissue is picked, i t goes through the process including microneedle contact, 
wiggle, tissue separation, and extraction. Actual images and path diagrams depicting 
the different stages of this process are presented in Figure 7.

extraction . B) The wiggle order and pattern of microneedle tip (diameter 40 µm) in one stage.

Figure 9 : The schematic diagram of microtissue collection arrangements after extraction 
by microneedle from two TLS areas in each sample . 
This method was applied to FFPE tissue sections from all samples. For each 
annotation type, a total of 10 picks from two different TLSs in each sample were 
pooled into a single deposit tube ( Figure 9). Afterward, optimized protocols were 
established to lyse tissues, capture mRNA by beads, and perform mRNA purification 
(shown in methods). 
4.5 mRNA pre -amplification requirements are assessed through post -picking 
qPCR 
Since the extracted mRNA exists in minute quantities, its concentration and quality 
cannot be measured directly without amplification. Therefore, it is necessary to run 
qPCR using highly expressed genes to assess the required number of cycles for pre -
amplification with the NanoString low RNA input Kit. A qPCR experiment was 
performed on collected mRNA samples using 3 primers: RPS13, GAPDH, and B2M.

## Page 63
 
63 
 Results showed that most samples with primer RPS13 and GAPDH exhibited normal 
amplification curves, with an average Ct value of 33.8 ( Figure 10 A i,ii). However, all 
samples using B2M as primer failed to amplify (Figure 10A iii), which is possibly due 
to issues with the primer design. Notably, all non -template control (NTC) displayed 
signals only near the baseline, confirming positive amplification of the samples 
(Figure 10A iv). The melt curve showed a clear single peak with Tm around 81°C for 
the RPS13 sample group, indicating specific and unique amplification (Figure 10 B i). 
However, the GADPH sample group had non -single peak signals, suggesting potential 
primer dimers and non -specific amplification (Figure 10B ii). 
In general, high but consistent Ct values from qPCR indicate the presence of 
extremely low levels of mRNA in the sample. Given that 10 cycles of pre -
amplification is typically recommended for FFPE samples with a minimum of 1 ng 
RNA input, the qPCR results suggest that all samples obtained by this method require 
testing for at least 14 -16 cycles to determine the appropriate pre -amplification cycles 
to conduct nCounter gene panel analysis . 
4.6 Nano -Pick outline 
This thesis developed Nano -pick, a novel method that can achieve precise microtissue 
extraction from FFPE samples and reliable mRNA collection for nCounter gene 
expression profiling (Figure 11 ). 
 
 
 
Figure 11: Diagram of Nano -Pick workflow .

This thesis developed Nano -pick, a novel method that can achieve precise microtissue 
extraction from FFPE samples and reliable mRNA collection for nCounter gene 
expression profiling (Figure 11 ). 
 
 
 
Figure 11: Diagram of Nano -Pick workflow . Adjacent sections from the same FFPE block 
were used for t -CycIF and microtissue picking respectively. Under the guidance of t -CycIF 
images, ROIs were selected and transferred to achieve precise picking . mRNA was extracted 
and validated in qPCR, meeting the requirements for Nanostring nCounter analysis . 
 


## Page 64
 
64 
 5 Discussion 
The rise of spatial transcriptomics allows researchers to access gene expression 
signatures at near -single-cell resolution. In cancer research, this new method offers 
deeper insights into the spatial structure and complex intercellular interactions of the 
TME, revealing its great potential in exploring anti -tumor immune response 
mechanisms and potential key regulatory genes. The latest mainstream technologies, 
such as Visium and Geomx, mainly rely on next -generation sequencing based on 
spatial barcoding to preserve spatial information (Moses & Pachter, 2022 ). However, 
despite high sequencing analysis costs, these methods cannot accurately capture the 
entire transcriptome of a specific cell subpopulation. Therefore, a simple, reliable, and 
sensitive spatially resolved transcriptional profiling method is needed with great 
research value. 
This master's thesis develops and validates a new method, Nano -Pick, which applies 
optimized microtissue extraction technology to FFPE samples and isolates mRNA 
from microtissues for nCounter gene expression analysis. Using optimized parameters 
and a semi -automated, high -precision microneedle operating platform, multiple preset

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0080
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
timized microtissue extraction technology to FFPE samples and isolates mRNA 
from microtissues for nCounter gene expression analysis. Using optimized parameters 
and a semi -automated, high -precision microneedle operating platform, multiple preset 
ROIs can be consistently collected with a success rate close to 100%. By innovatively 
modifying conventional immunostaining approaches, effective labeling of tumor cells, 
lymphocytes , and macrophages was achieved without antigen retrieval. This not only 
maximizes the mRNA integrity preservation but also upgrades the precision of the 
picking process. The ROIs recognition during picking is based on both tissue 
morphology and antibody fluorescence signals of specific cell types. This 
significantly improves the positioning accuracy for pre -selected ROIs and provides 
the feasibility of applying more complex R OIs in the future. 
Nano-Pick has optimized methods for low RNA amount extraction and has been 
proven to be completely viable for nCounter analysis after pre -amplification. The 
nCounter system has been widely used for transcriptome analysis due to its simple 
automated workflow and rapid analysis process (Kulkarni, 2011). The PanCancer 

## Page 65
 
65 
 gene panel provided by the nCounter system can detect the gene expression features 
of 770 genes (Cesano, 2015) , but the lack of sample spatial information limits its 
potential in cancer research. Therefore, Nano -Pick can provide multiple samples with 
unique spatial locations and cell subpopulations, enabling rapid spatially resolved 
transcriptome profiling by samp les or groups. On the one hand, cell type 
deconvolution is not necessary and the reliability of gene expression analysis for 
different cell subpop ulations is improved.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0081
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
populations, enabling rapid spatially resolved 
transcriptome profiling by samp les or groups. On the one hand, cell type 
deconvolution is not necessary and the reliability of gene expression analysis for 
different cell subpop ulations is improved. On the other hand, the potential high -
resolution single -cell level deconvolution algorithm for cell subtype inference can 
further realize spatial transcription analysis at single -cell resolution, giving Nano -pick 
incomparable developm ent potential. 
TLSs are the main local center containing immune cells in the TME, which is 
involved in activating and regulating anti -tumor immune responses. In this study, 
different cellular regions in TLSs were annotated separately, and multiple ROIs were 
selected amon g them. The successful picking demonstrates the ability of Nano -Pick to 
explore critical microstructures such as TLSs in FFPE tissues . This can further 
facilitate the exploration of the spatial attraction and interactions of different immune 
cell subtypes in TLSs, as well as the screening of highly expressed cytokines in its 
unique gene expression profile . Given that FFPE tissues are easily available for 
clinical cancer research and can be stored for years, this novel method holds promise 
for accessing histopathology archives and contributing to translational research . 
Therefore, it is possible to conduct large -scale spatially resolved transcriptome 
research with a large cohort of archived FFPE samples without the time constraints of 
sample collection. 
It must be acknowledged that this study has some shortcomings. Although Nano -Pick 
has been fully validated and is viable for nCounter gene panel analysis, only qPCR 
experiments were performed on multiple collected mRNA samples to initially verify

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0082
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
t must be acknowledged that this study has some shortcomings. Although Nano -Pick 
has been fully validated and is viable for nCounter gene panel analysis, only qPCR 
experiments were performed on multiple collected mRNA samples to initially verify 
the feasibility of the entire workflow. The quality control of mRNA and the number of 
pre-amplification cycles in nCounter analysis need to be further determined. Finally, 

## Page 66
 
66 
 many antibodies could be useful if validated to achieve satisfactory staining results 
without antigen retrieval. I aim to conduct m ore validation experiments to establish a 
standardized antibody library that can label different cell types during the picking 
process. It will further expand the application of Nano -Pick in other research areas. 
Nonetheless, this study innovatively establishes all experiment protocols of Nano-
Pick based on existing experimental methods . It not only provides a new approach for 
spatial transcription al analysis of FFPE samples, but also provides the possibility of 
other high -throughput analyses. Since microtissue extraction relies on simple and 
robust microneedles, it allows for consistent and accurate mechanical isolation and 
collection of microtissue from target areas. These microtissues contain complete 
whole transcriptomes, genomes , and proteom es, enabling highly sensitive multi -omics 
analysis including gene expression profiling. Given that the development of spatial 
genomics is still limited by the number of single -cell DNA copies, the development of 
new artificial intelligence -based computational methods will better utilize Nano -Pick 
to promote quantitative research in spatial genomics. 
The huge potential of Nano -Pick should not be ignored, as the development of

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
opies, the development of 
new artificial intelligence -based computational methods will better utilize Nano -Pick 
to promote quantitative research in spatial genomics. 
The huge potential of Nano -Pick should not be ignored, as the development of 
corresponding automated procedures will extend its current capabilities significantly. 
After completing the calibration and ROIs selection, the picking process is actually a 
very tedious and repetitive process. Therefore, it is entirely possible that this process 
can be automated, which will greatly improve the efficiency of microtissue extraction. 
In addition, the success rate of picking is influenced by variations in tissue secti on 
thickness across different areas, which is also the main reason for extraction failure. 
One possible solution is to use advanced instruments to pre -measure the thickness 
distribution of entire tissue sections and integrate this data with all other saved 
information of each ROI. This will effectively facilitate the individual correction of 
each ROI during the picking process to ensure successful extraction. 
Furthermore, there is ongoing advancement in computational image analysis of t -
CycIF images that enables semi-automated cell type identification (Schapiro et al., 

## Page 67
 
67 
 2022; Kang et al., 2024 ). These computational pipelines will improve the annotation 
of target tissue regions and the selection of ROIs for the Nano -Pick method in the 
future. Such semi-automated cell type identification can effectively eliminate errors in 
human annotation and greatly improve the accuracy of the picking for different cell 
populations. Another prospect is to use Nano -Pick to extract microtissues from

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf::chunk_0084
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
. Such semi-automated cell type identification can effectively eliminate errors in 
human annotation and greatly improve the accuracy of the picking for different cell 
populations. Another prospect is to use Nano -Pick to extract microtissues from 
multiple consecutive F FPE sections of specific regions such as GC, and create a 3D 
gene expression map of the target tissue region. This will break through the two -
dimensional limitations of current spatial transcriptomic research and provide new 
possibilities for cancer research. 
In summary, this study developed Nano-Pick, a novel method for spatially resolved 
gene expression analysis based on precise microtissue extraction and the nCounter 
system. Nano-Pick can promote higher-resolution spatial analysis and interactions of 
immune cell populations in the TME . It also holds promise for translational research 
across diverse diseases, thereby advancing the prospects of precision medicine .

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Thesis Plan Abstract.pdf::chunk_0000
