vector_chunks.text:
## Page 1
Nano -Pick: A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of 
Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer 
 
Thesis Plan Abstract 
Ovarian cancer ranks as one of the deadliest forms of gynecological malignancies, 
affecting over 300,000 women globally each year. High -grade serous carcinoma 
(HGSC), the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer, typically 
presents with a poor prognosis. Due to the absence of distinct symptoms in the early 
stages and the high dissemination rate of HGSC, 75% of HGSC patients are 
diagnosed at stage III or IV according to the FIGO staging system . Despite extensive 
efforts, optimal cytoreduction alone with various combinations of chemotherapy 
cannot significantly improve the survival rate of patients with advanced HGSC . 
Recently, although advancements have been made in targeted therapies for ovarian 
cancer, including the use of PARP inhibitors and bevacizumab, immunotherapies have 
not achieved the same level of success in treating this disease. This can be attributed 
to the high heterogeneity and unique immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment 
(TME) of ovarian cancer . The complexity of immune cell interactions and regulatory 
immune networks among different cell types, including T cells, B cells, macrophages, 
dendritic cells, and myeloid -derived suppressor cells (MDSCs), underscores the 
complexity of the immune landscape in cancer. Further exploration and understanding 
of components of the TME could illuminate mechanisms by which tumors evade 
immune surveillance and exploit immune checkpoints to suppress effective anti -tumor 
immunity . 
Tertiary lymphoid structure (TLS) is a spontaneously formed, lymph node -like

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Thesis Plan Abstract.pdf::chunk_0001
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Thesis Plan Abstract.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
components of the TME could illuminate mechanisms by which tumors evade 
immune surveillance and exploit immune checkpoints to suppress effective anti -tumor 
immunity . 
Tertiary lymphoid structure (TLS) is a spontaneously formed, lymph node -like 
aggregate of diverse immune cells within non -lymphoid tissues. The formation of 
TLS in tumor area is initiated by a cascade of inflammatory signals within the TME , 
including cytokines (like TNF -α, IL-6) and chemokines (such as CCL19, CCL21, 
CXCL13) . As TLS matures, there are similar compartmentalized structures with 
lymph nodes containing B -cell zones featuring germinal centers (GC), T -cell zones, 
and high endothelial venules (HEVs). Tertiary lymphoid structures (TLS) have been 
shown to significantly contribute to tumor adaptive immunity by enhancing T cell 
activation and assisting in antigen presentation . However, although research indicates 
that patients rich in TLS will experience a better prognosis, some studies have also 
highlighted the potential role of TLS in the immunosuppressive process facilitating 
the escape of cancer cells from immune surveillance . Considering the double -edged 
sword role of TLS in anti -tumor immunity, the analysis of spatially resolved gene 

## Page 2
expression in both naive and mature TLS areas using clinical samples holds 
significant potential and value. 
In this study, I plan to develop a novel analysis methodology by integrating 
microtissue region extraction technology (Rarecyte) with the nCounter® gene panel 
analysis system (Nanostring) to achieve spatially resolved gene expression profiling 
of TLS regions in clinical ovarian cancer FFPE samples. To achieve this , I need to 
first verify whether a series of antibody products can bind to target antigens and have

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Thesis Plan Abstract.pdf::chunk_0002
vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Thesis Plan Abstract.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ystem (Nanostring) to achieve spatially resolved gene expression profiling 
of TLS regions in clinical ovarian cancer FFPE samples. To achieve this , I need to 
first verify whether a series of antibody products can bind to target antigens and have 
stable fluorescence signal expression without heat -induced antigen retrieval methods 
(HIER). Besides, I also need to test and find optimal parameters to implement a 
feasible microtissue extraction method for FFPE sections of ovarian cancer tissue. 
Based on these experiments, a protocol can be established to achieve stable and 
reliable tissue digestion and micro -mRNA extraction with test sample slides . Since 
only about 100 -200 cells are deposited in each tube during the microtissue extraction 
process, the amount of mRNA extracted is very small, and qPCR is required to verify 
the required number of amplification cycles. Before real picking experiments, t issue -
based cyclic immunofluorescence (t -CycIF) will be perfor med on the adjacent slides 
to help with the selection of ready -to-pick microtissue regions. In the final phase, 
samples that meet the quality control standards will undergo analysis using the 
nCounter gene expression panel to discover spatially resolved gene expression 
patterns.

Perustelut arvosanalle
Motivering
Justification
The Master's thesis titled "Nano-Pick: A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression
 
Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer" submitted by Zhihan Liang addresses the
 
development of a novel methodology applied to ovarian cancer samples to study cellular diversity in
 
tertiary lymphoid structures (TLSs). Throughout the thesis, the student demonstrates a deep
 
understanding of the existing literature and a remarkable ability to apply and combine different
 
methodological protocols in a timely and relevant manner.
The novel technique introduced in this thesis allows precise microtissue extraction and spatial gene
 
expression profiling. This method is particularly notable for its application to formalin-fixed paraffin-
embedded (FFPE) samples, overcoming common challenges with innovative tissue cyclic
 
immunofluorescence (t-CycIF) and optimized immunostaining techniques.
The study design is well-structured, and the methods used are appropriate for addressing the research
 
aims proposed. Although it is clear that the project will continue, the student is aware of the current
 
limitations and clearly outlines the next steps.
In conclusion, Zhihan Liang's thesis represents a valuable contribution to the field of translational
 
medicine. The work demonstrates not only a solid theoretical understanding but also advanced technical

ons and clearly outlines the next steps.
In conclusion, Zhihan Liang's thesis represents a valuable contribution to the field of translational
 
medicine. The work demonstrates not only a solid theoretical understanding but also advanced technical
 
and analytical skills. I recommend that it be awarded the maximum grade (5), recognizing the effort and
 
quality of the work presented.

## Slide 15
Ovarian Cancer
8th most common cancer for women
HGSC most lethal subtype
Limited treatment options
 🡪 primary/interval debulking surgery
 🡪 (neo)adjuvant chemotherapy
 🡪 PARP inhibitors
Low response rates for immune checkpoint inhibitors
‹#›
Speaker notes: Ovarian Cancer – why is it relevant?

## Slide 16
Tumor Microenvironment
promotes
 Tumor survival
 Tumor development
 Metastasis
 Immune evasion

🡪 interesting therapeutic target
‹#›
Speaker notes: for an integrated spatial and proteomic exploration of the TME

Tumor microenvironment 
- heterologous environment 
plays a vital role within tumor survival, development, metastasis, immune evasion
And is therefore an interesting therapeutic target
Exploration necessary, since understanding mechanisms and interactions of tumor with its surrounding cells and how tumor recruits those cells could help to find new treatment approaches

That’s why:

And is therefore an interesting therapeutic target
Exploration necessary, since understanding mechanisms and interactions of tumor with its surrounding cells and how tumor recruits those cells could help to find new treatment approaches

That’s why:
Spatial resolution methods are crucial in tumor research as they enable the precise visualization and analysis of the tumor microenvironment (TME), revealing the spatial organization of tumor cells, immune cells, stromal components, and extracellular factors. These techniques help uncover key interactions driving tumor progression, metastasis, and immune evasion, ultimately guiding the development of targeted therapies and personalized treatment strategies.

## Slide 17
Spatial-resolution Methods
Mass cytometry/spectrometry-based imaging methods🡪 high multiplexing, expensive equipment, complex, time consuming

Fluorescent-based imaging methods🡪limited multiplexing, cheap, AB-panel based, fast
‹#›
Speaker notes: 1. Mass Cytometry/Spectrometry-Based Imaging
These include imaging mass cytometry (IMC), multiplexed ion beam imaging (MIBI), and related mass spectrometry-based imaging methods.
2. Fluorescent Staining-Based Imaging
These include standard immunofluorescence (IF), confocal microscopy, STED, and multiplexed fluorescence imaging (e.g., CODEX, IBEX, CyCIF).
🡪 method is lacking for dissection of specific regions of interest and getting spatial and full proteomics insight of small regions in an affordable, customizable, simple way

## Slide 18

no method that provides spatial & FULL proteomic insight for small regions
🡪 simple, affordable & customizable
Why is it needed?

provides spatial & FULL proteomic insight for small regions

## Slide 19
Resources of the Lab
Collection of >500 clinical ovarian cancer samples 
Highly multiplexed tissue-based Cyclic ImmunoFluorescence (tCycIF) imaging
Rarecyte CyteFinder II imaging instrument
Established computational infrastructure & image processing pipeline
‹#›
Speaker notes: Collection of clinical ovarian cancer samples which are collected as part of the ONCOSYS-OVA at the Helsinki Hospital 🡪 formalin fixed, paraffin embedded tissue samples
tCycIF: immuno staining + DNA dye, imaging, fluorophore bleaching
Rarecyte CyteFinder II = high-speed, whole slide imaging systems with options for multiplexed liquid biopsy and tissue spatial analysis
Computational infrastructure & imaging pipeline for image processing (illumination correction, stitching, segmentation, quanitification for single cell data, phenotyping)

## Slide 20
ProteoPicking
combination of t-CycIF and MS-based proteomics analysis
dissection of regions of interest
structural, functional and proteomic landscape
high spatial precision
‹#›
Speaker notes: The mass spectrometry analysis of the picked cell samples was performed by the Turku Proteomics facility, a unit of the Turku Bioscience Centre, University of Turku.The samples were analysed using a bottom-up approach, following the facility’s established protocols. The used method was data independent acquisition shot gun proteomics via liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS).

**AICDA_1**

Slides: 7_S067_iOme_4; 7_S128_iOme_5+6

Date: 11.01.2025

Dilution: 1:50, 1:200, 1:1000

Exposure time: 0.3s

AICDA (Activation-induced cytidine deaminase) is essential for:

- Somatic Hypermutation (SHM): Introduces point mutations into the
 variable regions of immunoglobulin (Ig) genes, increasing antibody
 diversity.

- Class Switch Recombination (CSR): Facilitates the switching of
 antibody isotypes (e.g., IgM to IgG, IgA, or IgE), allowing
 antibodies to mediate different immune functions.

It acts by deaminating cytosine bases in DNA to uracil, which is
recognized and processed by DNA repair pathways, leading to targeted
mutations or recombination events.

AICDA is primarily expressed in germinal center B cells during
activation and proliferation. It can be found in Activated B cells in
secondary lymphoid organs (e.g., lymph nodes, spleen, tonsils) + TLSs.
AICDA is not expressed in naïve or memory B cells or other immune cell
types under normal conditions.

![](media/image47.png){width="1.9972911198600174in"
height="3.3408409886264216in"}

1:50 Yellow AICDA, Purple CD138

# PROJECT PLAN {#project-plan .unnumbered}

## Atlas of TLS and TILs in ovarian cancer {#atlas-of-tls-and-tils-in-ovarian-cancer .unnumbered}

Descriptive introduction from our cohort where we explore the presence
and basic metrics of distinct cancer types.

### QUESTIONS: {#questions .unnumbered}

- How do TLS and TIL characteristics vary across different ovarian
 > cancer histologies (e.g., HGSC, LGSC, clear cell carcinoma,
 > carcinosarcoma)?

- How does the characterization and spatial organization of TLS and
 > TILs correlate with clinical outcomes, specifically
 > progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS), in
 > these cancer patients?

### TASKS: {#tasks .unnumbered}

#### Select the cohort and extract the clinical information

#### Create manual annotations for HE selecting the GC, immune aggregates 

#### HE exploration analyses at different levels:

- Diagnosis and histologies (HGSC, LGSC, clear cell,
 > carcinosarcomas...)

- Tissue type

- Therapy sequence (PDS, IDS)

- Molecular profiles

\(1\) classify samples based on presence of immune aggregates or
germinal centers

> \(2\) Explore the TIL/TLS diversity across diagnoses, tissue sites,
> therapies and molecular profiles
>
> \(3\) Preliminar spatial analyses on HE, such as exploring the
> proximity of TLS/TILs to the tumor, metrics of aggregation of immune
> cells
>
> \(4\) explore the clinical associations of these with the PFS and OS

#### Bulk RNA-seq analysis of the iCAN samples: 

- Calculate the 12-Chemokine signature and compare across histologies,
 > correlate with the HE annotations

- Perform DEG analysis (same code as with prior manuscript)

#### Plan a large tCycIF experiment (\~50 samples) according to our results

- Calculate the 12-Chemokine signature and compare across histologies,
 > correlate with the HE annotations

- Perform DEG analysis (same code as with prior manuscript)

#### Plan a large tCycIF experiment (\~50 samples) according to our results

- Subset a set of samples from the cohort that represent the distinct
 > groups

- Select, order, and validate an antibody panel

- Perform the experiments

- Post-processing of the images

- Spatial analyses

## Exploration of the TME's link to TLS/TIL appearance {#exploration-of-the-tmes-link-to-tlstil-appearance .unnumbered}

In this section we will funnel the information from the prior atlas to
something more concrete, specific diagnosis (HGSC/Endometrioid), tissue
type (Omentum), molecular profile (HRP) and distinct therapy sequences
(PDS/IDS). We want to explore the patterns within the TME correlating
with presence of these distinct aggregates, as well as profiling the
spatial interactions of these structures.

### QUESTIONS: {#questions-1 .unnumbered}

- Do TILs and TLS-containing samples differ spatially and
 > functionally? Why do samples display heterogeneity in TLS and GC
 > presence?

 - *Based on preliminary analysis;* Is the presence of GC a
 > prognostic marker, and if so which are the mechanisms that
 > promote or impair their development

- Are TIL and TLS presence linked to specific microenvironmental
 > features or structural patterns? How might these patterns
 > influence the clinical outcomes?

### TASKS {#tasks-1 .unnumbered}

#### Perform a new experiment OR recycle data from prior experiments with adequate morphology markers

 > features or structural patterns? How might these patterns
 > influence the clinical outcomes?

b. Annotate the resulting clusters and observe if any of those

TIL and TLS presence, aiming to uncover mechanistic insights and
potential biomarkers.

 > were divided into overlapping tiles, processed individually,
 > and reassembled with label harmonization using a custom untile
 > function. To exclude background regions, a Gaussian Mixture
 > Model (GMM) was applied to the mean intensities of each tile,
 > and the lowest-intensity cluster was automatically classified
 > as background. Segmentation focused on nuclear compartments
 > using DAPI and optional membrane markers, with all predictions

##### QUANTIFICATION {#quantification .unnumbered}

Quantification of the segmented data was conducted with an in-house
analysis pipeline running on a Windows workstation.

Issue:

- **Quantification was getting stuck**(June, reported by Zhihan):

 - The quantification issue was resolved by identifying that the
 > problem was with the mask saving format, not the
 > quantification script. The Mesmer mask was saved with uint16,
 > which caused duplicated CellIDs when there were more than
 > 65,535 unique cells. The solution is to save the mask in
 > uint32 (which supports up to 4.2 billion values).

 - To avoid rerunning Mesmer, a script was created by Ada to
 > relabel the CellIDs in the existing masks. The relabeling
 > function can be found
 > [[here]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/TLS/blob/main/tCycIF/image-processing/convert_mask_to_uint32.ipynb),
 > and the process was tested with no issues and ran on a loop by
 > Zhihan. The quantification was successfully performed on the
 > relabeled mask.

 - The quantification script used is located at
 > F:\\farkkilab2\\7_TLS\\Data_analysis (quantification_workflow)
 > and ran without problems on the workstation, taking 17 minutes
 > using 7 cores (June, Ada)

##### NOISE REDUCTION {#noise-reduction .unnumbered}

**0.65 microns/pixel, binned images**

Ada realized some channels like CD20 or CD4 could not be gated since
there were not big differences between the signal from artifacts and
background with actual positive cells. To be able to use these images,
these faulty or noisy channels were corrected with white-top hat
filtering in python (script: Noise reduction). After the correction in
the affected channels, UNIFORM was ran and the corresponding values were
integrated in the CellIDs that passed the QC.

- Filtered images were stored in /tif/filtered_images, with subfolders

## H) GeoMx {#h-geomx .unnumbered}

### Geomx Experiment {#geomx-experiment .unnumbered}

The experiment was done in Feb and March 2025. 30 samples were shared
with the EyeMT project and finally 28 samples passed QC for GeoMx.

Protocols are saved here
[[GeoMx]{.underline}](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1JtjMpnAPUM6-O3FQ39PEuolsvUWy7eFB?usp=sharing)

#### In a nutshell {#in-a-nutshell .unnumbered}

Formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue sections (5 µm) were
sectioned adjacent to tCycIF slides. These sections are then processed
according to the manufacturer's instructions for the GeoMx Digital
Spatial Profiler (DSP; Bruker Spatial Biology, MAN-10150-06). Briefly,
slides were baked overnight, deparaffinized, and rehydrated before
target retrieval in 1× Tris-EDTA (pH 9.0) using a steamer at \~99 °C,
followed by Proteinase K digestion at 37 °C to expose RNA targets.
Sections were then postfixed and hybridized overnight with the GeoMx RNA
Probe Mix containing UV-cleavable oligonucleotide tags. After two
stringent washes, customized morphology markers and nuclear stains were
applied to enable ROI visualization.

Slides were scanned on the GeoMx DSP instrument to acquire whole slide
fluorescence morphology images, and regions of interest (ROIs) were
selected for illumination. Upon UV exposure, oligonucleotide tags were

e
applied to enable ROI visualization.

Slides were scanned on the GeoMx DSP instrument to acquire whole slide
fluorescence morphology images, and regions of interest (ROIs) were
selected for illumination. Upon UV exposure, oligonucleotide tags were
released from each ROI and aspirated into a collection plate for library
preparation. Libraries were PCR-amplified using GeoMx Seq Code primers,
pooled, purified, QCed and sequenced on Novaseq X 10B 100c following the
GeoMx NGS Readout User Manual (MAN-10153). FASTQ files were processed
using the GeoMx NGS Pipeline to generate digital count conversion (DCC)
files.

#### ROI selection {#roi-selection .unnumbered}

ROIs from adjacent t-CyCIF images were pre-selected based on TLS immune
markers: CD20, CD21, CD4, CD8a, BCL6, Ki67, and Vimentin.

For mature TLS regions, three Regions of Interest (ROIs) were selected:
one located at the germinal center, one in the T-cell and B-cell regions
adjacent to the germinal center, and one in the stroma region near the
mature TLS. These three ROIs were collectively referred to as a group of
ROIs, each marked with a specific numerical label to distinguish it from
the others. This ROI selection method was adopted for each selected
mature or near-mature TLS region. All mature or near-mature TLS regions
in the sample were selected as much as possible, and ROIs belonging to
near-mature TLS were further subdivided in subsequent data processing.

In addition, five immune aggregate regions and corresponding five
Regions of Interest (ROIs) were selected for each sample. These immune
aggregates may be located in the early stages of TLS development.

The selection of ROIs referenced pre-selected ROIs on adjacent tcycif
slices by loading a tcycif image containing the pre-selected ROIs and

ere selected for each sample. These immune
aggregates may be located in the early stages of TLS development.

The selection of ROIs referenced pre-selected ROIs on adjacent tcycif
slices by loading a tcycif image containing the pre-selected ROIs and
overlaying it onto the actual scanned geomx image in the geomx system.
However, due to slight deformations in adjacent slices, the actual
selected ROIs and the pre-selected ROIs were not entirely identical. The
final selected ROIs must actually correspond to TLS regions or LAgg
regions in the Geomx image. However, due to the poor staining effect of
CD20 on geomx sections, this selection relied more heavily on the
results of CD45.

Details on ROIs can be found here:

[[GeoMx_P9_EyeMTbatch3_P7_TLS_1.xlsx]{.underline}](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1copNEOCLrFFzoN8L73GupVir-I4lXB7i/edit?gid=1286186248#gid=1286186248)

Final GeoMx ROIs were saved on the transformed PNG image.

#### Experiment details {#experiment-details .unnumbered}

01-03/2025

All experiment procedures, details and corresponding notes can be found
here:

[[GeoMx_P9_EyeMTbatch3_P7_TLS_1.xlsx]{.underline}](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1copNEOCLrFFzoN8L73GupVir-I4lXB7i/edit?gid=1286186248#gid=1286186248)

After the experiment, the collected ROIs underwent library preparation
and were then sent to FuGu for TapeStation DNA Quality Control. The

#### GC signature scores {#gc-signature-scores .unnumbered}

I defined signature gene sets representing germinal center (GC) B cells,
T--B zone cells, stromal components, plasma cells, and aggregate
regions, and quantified their expression across annotated TLS subtypes
using the Harmony-corrected GeoMx dataset. Through boxplot and violin
plot visualization, I assessed spatial differences in key marker
expression among TLS categories (Stroma, T--B zone, Mature_GC,
Immature_GC, LAgg).

Generated boxplots and violin plots showing the distribution of marker
gene expression across TLS categories. Statistical testing included
Kruskal--Wallis and pairwise Wilcoxon tests to evaluate significant
differences between TLS subtypes (e.g., Immature_GC vs Agg, Agg vs GC).

- Defined two Milky Spot (MS) gene sets:

 - **MS-up genes:** *CD163, MRC1, FABP4, APOL1, IL10RA* (macrophage
 > and stromal enrichment).

 - **MS-down genes:** *BCL6, MKI67, PDCD1, PDPN, IL6* (germinal
 > center or proliferative features).

- Calculated **MS-up**, **MS-down**, and **combined (MS-up −
 > MS-down)** scores per AOI.

 > structures.

#### New TLS label {#new-tls-label .unnumbered}

##### Label Reassignment and Confidence Estimation {#label-reassignment-and-confidence-estimation .unnumbered}

- For each AOI, determine the top and second-highest signature scores.

- Calculated a **confidence metric** = (top − second) / top.

- AOIs with confidence \< 0.5 were labeled as *"Intermediate"*,
 > suggesting ambiguous transcriptional states.

- Reassigned labels where appropriate, preserving "Agg" classification
 > for AOIs originally annotated as aggregates but enriched in TB or
 > Stromal genes.

 > temporal transitions during TLS evolution.

MiXCR was used to perform B-cell receptor (BCR) repertoire
reconstruction from bulk RNA-seq data. The standard workflow consisted
of:

3. Exporting clone tables (mixcr exportClones)

selected samples (e.g., OVCA1).

------Suggesting more B cells in the TME?

-- After B cells mature, they produce a large amount of immunoglobulin
mRNA (especially in plasma cells), with a single cell having thousands
of transcripts. T cell TCR mRNA levels are relatively low (only 1--2
TCRα/β mRNAs per T cell).

-- Bulk RNA-seq captures transcripts with high expression levels. BCR
transcripts (especially IgM/IgG) are usually much higher than TCR
transcripts, so more clones are detected.

Post-processing was done at both individual and overlap (combined)
levels to explore clonotype diversity and overlap across samples.

## J）Scellex BCR Pilot {#jscellex-bcr-pilot .unnumbered}

### SCellex technology {#scellex-technology .unnumbered}

SCellex develops a spatial single-cell gene expression analysis platform
based on visual barcodes.

Their core technology is a **visualized barcode-based Picowell
microarray**, combined with AI image analysis, enabling the direct
capture of RNA on tissue slices or single-cell suspensions while
preserving spatial location information.

- Random barcodes combined with image localization can achieve spatial
 > resolution capabilities close to those of a single cell.

The goal is to perform high-throughput multi-omics sequencing while
maintaining spatial context (i.e., cell location within the tissue).

The plan is to first test spatial methods with PolyA captured for Fresh
Frozen. Theoretically, this has the potential to capture RNA targets on
FFPE and combine them with a BCR/TCR specific RNA panel for spatially
localized BCR analysis. The technology platform supports multi-omics and

hods with PolyA captured for Fresh
Frozen. Theoretically, this has the potential to capture RNA targets on
FFPE and combine them with a BCR/TCR specific RNA panel for spatially
localized BCR analysis. The technology platform supports multi-omics and
functional assays, and can be further integrated with proteomics in the
future.
